item.author: RaNa Atef
item.p: An influential name in the underground Doom Metal scene, the band manages to establish its unique sound of sorrow, and its grieving, aching rhythms. Mostly of Doom Metal lovers initiated their listening career by listening to "Wish", "Eternal Autumn", "The Silent Cry". And after a 10 year of absence, Forest of Shadows is back with a new strong album "Among the Dormant Watchers". Today, I had the honor to interview this great band, the band who influence me to go deeper into Doom Metal music.

Thank you guys for giving me this chance, and to make my dream of chatting with you come true. Firstly, I want to start with the gap between "Six Waves of Woe", and "Among the Dormant Watchers". Where have you been?

I actually started working on material for what would become âAmong the Dormant Watchersâ just after âSix Waves of Woeâ was released but the progress was slow. The following years it actually almost came to a stall because of other things like family and work. Then it all took a turn. I updated my gear and returned to the material with the determination that it would become the next full length album, which apparently it did.
Is there any concept story behind your latest album?

Not really. The instrumental part of the album was composed over a period of many years and each songs has its own backstory and set of influences. The lyrics on the other hand do share some similarities between songs. For instance most of them are inspired by the state of the world and feed on the atrocities and darkness that is out there and the guilt and shame of being a part of it all. Still, not really a concept and nothing that goes for all songs but I guess itâs as close as it gets.
I want to go back to the idea of forming the band, How did you choose the name of the band "Forest of Shadows"

I was a big fan of Opeth at the time and they had a song on their first album called âForest of Octoberâ. I guess I liked the title and simply swapped october for shadows to make it a bit more dark and doomy. Thatâs basically it. Later on I found out that âForest of Shadows â was a common name for places in role playing and computer games but that didnât influence me in picking the name. At that time you didnât really google the name to see if it was taken.
What are your main musical influences in general?

When I founded the band it was mostly classic doom/death metal but over the years I have moved more in a direction of post rock/metal, black metal and all kinds of dark music that take it to extremes. They all influence in different ways and there isnât one type of music, or a single band or album that I use as style guide for everything. For lyrics I normally donât get influenced by other bands lyrics at all but rather use books or, as mentioned earlier, the state of the world, normally as told by various documentaries.
"Six Waves of Woe" is one of the greatest Doom Metal releases ever! Specially "Selfdestructive", please tell us more about this album and the process of writing it

Thanks! I also find âSelfdestructiveâ one of the best song on that album. So, while âDepartureâ was the first album I did all by myself, or at least completed by myself, âSix Waves of Woeâ was the album where I learnt from my mistakes and perfected my visions, or at least took it all to the next level. There was more confidence which resulted in more focus on composing and recording and less on tweaking the sound and editing recordings. I also knew from day one how it all should sound and used the same references during the entire production. In terms of lyrics I started to move away from the classic doom metal thematics, something that has continued on âAmong the Dormant Watchersâ. Instead I found new inspiration in real life events and the true darkness that is out there instead of over dramatic novels and poetry.
Why does the musician drive his inner feelings into sad, gloomy music?

Catharsis I guess. You need a way to ventilate your inner darkness and for me it has always been through listening and composing dark music. Somehow those feelings give the songs an extra dimension and more meaning. It makes each and every song personal and something I can relate to.
Is there any live performances soon?

No. Previous to âWhere Dreams turn to Dustâ we did two shows and that was during a time when there was a âweâ in the band. It was fun but it never did the songs justice. I sometimes play with the idea of doing some live shows with guest musicians but Iâm quite confident it will never happen.
What do you think of your Middle Eastern fans? Are you on contact with them?

They write and I answer, just like fans from other parts of the world. I really donât care too much where my fans come from. Still, for some mysterious reason the majority of my fans do seem to come from the Middle East and I still struggle to get why? So, maybe I should pay those fans some extra attention.
What are the most difficulties that face underground Metal bands?

As of today, acting in Sweden there isnât really any difficulties. I guess the fact that there is so much music out there could be an issue as it makes it harder for your own stuff to be relevant if you havenât picked a very unique style. I am perfectly aware of that the situation is different in other countries, especially some of the less free countries in the middle east.
How do you the see Doom Metal scene now?

Quite diverged. There are so many different styles and directions where some share very little so for me itâs not that relevant to talk about the Doom Metal scene at all. Iâm not even sure if my own music classify as Doom Metal anymore. I quite often find music that is doomy sorted under Black Metal or Post Rock/Metal and when I browse the stuff sorted as Doom I quite often get disappointed.
What are your plans, and dreams you want to fulfill?
For music, to keep making relevant music and to continue to perfect the visions I have for Forest of Shadows. Being a DIY artist there is always room for improvement.

item.author: Aleksey Evdokimov
item.p: Hail Niclas! Actually I didnât expect any news from Forest Of Shadows for years, so Iâm surprised with news that your third album Among The Dormant Watchers is about to release through Inverse Records. What did make you return to Forest after ten years of silence?

I actually never left the forest, I just got lost in there for a while. The main reason why I awoke was probably because I was iterating on the songs that would later on end up on âAmong the Dormant Watchersâ in a very, very slow pace during the first five years and it felt that I wasnât getting anywhere while I still felt that the songs had great potential. So, I upgraded my recording gear and simply started working more focused with a determination that the songs should end up on some album. During this time Firebox went out of business so during a period there wasnât really a label to release the album but I ignored that and focused on the music. After some struggle with other labels that showed interest but didnât deliver, Inverse Records showed up as the saviour and offered to release the album.
Your first full-length Departure was released in 2004, then Six Waves Of Woe appeared in 2008, but then you suddenly stop, was it a kind of creative crisis or something else?

Family and work took all focus for a couple of years and even though I never really gave up on my music I simply wasnât giving it much attention. The creativity was there all the time but the time and energy to channel it into my music simply wasnât.

Six Waves Of Woe continues the theme of debut album, so what about Among The Dormant Watchers? Could it be a strict successor of Six Waves after all these years? How much of old Forest Of Shadows left in new material?

There is much similarity between the two albums but I do explore some new territory on the new album. Regarding old material there is actually one song, I wonât say which, that goes back to times before Forest of Shadows. It has been reworked quite a lot though but if you knew the original song you would recognize the melody. The rest of the songs were all done after Six Waves of Woe although I tried to perfect things I started off on that album.
How actually long did you work over these songs? Was it a fast process as a sudden wave of inspiration or did you take your time and work without hurry?

They were worked on for almost ten years but as mentioned, during some of these years I didnât focus too much on making music so effectively maybe five years. I did not hurry. The only thing that was done pretty fast was the lyrics and vocals, at least in relation to the rest.
What kind of topics do you explore in your new songs? Is it general Forest Of Shadows idea the same?

I try to move away from cliche thematics and find topics that are not just overdramatized romantic nonsense but real darkness and pain. It makes it all feel so much more relevant and authentic and gives the songs much more depth and meaning.

Probably, I wouldnât ask you previous question, but thereâs a songs titled âDogs Of Chernobylââ¦ Whatâs it about? Actually I have a guessâ¦ I remember you did work in PC games industry, so I can suppose that the song is connected with S.T.A.L.K.E.R. video gameâ¦

I do work in the game development industry but the kind of games I work on are not in any way related to my music. The songs is actually inspired by stories told in the book Voices from Chernobyl by nobel prize winner Svetlana Aleksijevitj. Itâs one of the most tragic books I have read and there is an episode about the dogs that were abandoned by their owners during the catastrophe and their tragic demise.
Once you recorded a version of Swedish traditional song, do you have something similar this time?

We did play a Swedish traditional song during one of the two gigs we did when the band was not a solo act but we never recorded that one or any other traditional song. Still, some of the songs of Forest of Shadows, especially on the new album, are heavily inspired, at least in terms of melody, by swedish folk music.
Forest Of Shadows always was your solo project, did something change this time? Did someone else help you at the studio?

Not always, during When Dreams Turn To Dust and its previous two demos I was running the band with Micce Andersson and during a short period there were other members as well. I guess there were times when the other members saw it all as my one man show as Iâm not the best at compromising and it was doomed to end up as a one man show in the end. Everything on the new album was done by me apart from mastering. Itâs not the smartest way to do it but at least it gives me full control and allows the music to be as true to my visions as possibly can.
Niclas, where did you record Among The Dormant Watchers? As I understand first records were done in your home studio, so whatâs now? And what kind of equipment did you use for this recording?

Just like before, nothing has been recorded in a proper studio but instead at random locations. Apart from instruments and microphones I have a mobile studio consisting of a laptop and an external sound card which allows me to record basically anywhere. For instance, I try to record most vocals where I donât need to care about irritating my neighbors. The rest I do at home, in the living room, at the dinner table, in the basement, wherever it works and wherever I can gets things done. I have been doing this for such a long time that I can record really depressing music while the kids are playing all around me. That being said I donât get inspired to write depressing music by kids having a good time but once I have the inspiration and the music in my head it doesnât really matter if I record it all during nighttime in a desolate forest or in suburbia during daytime.
You also did run project Ningizzia, is it still on hold or did you finally put it to rest?

Yes, me and Stephane Peudupin used run that band. It was very much of a project or even experiment considering that we did it all over the net during times when internet wasnât really what internet is today. We havenât really put it rest for the simple reason that it wasnât really a band in the classical meaning but more of a collaboration and as I said project or experiment. We still keep contact and we might record something someday or maybe we donât. I think we both want to do something but none of us have enough time to spend, at least not right now.
Tenebra! I am glad to make an interview with you, i like very dark and doom music like yours. Can you tell us about your past, to let us know something about you?

I founded Forest of Shadows as a solo-project in the autumn of 1997. After having recorded the demo "the silent cry" I teamed up with multi-instrumentalist Micce Andersson. We reinterpreted and polished up the old material and released it as promotion material both on cd and online at mp3.com, all with the intention of attracting a record deal. Putting songs on mp3.com turned out to be a winning concept and for a couple of weeks we dominated the metal charts there. The english company "rage of achilles" (cult of luna, mistress) had noted this and offered us a record deal. In 2001 the mCD "where dreams turn to dust" was released and soon afterwards the work on a full-length album began. During its production, me and Micce started drifting apart, not as friends but in terms of musical direction. This resulted in that I had to finish the album, later on titled "departure", on my own. As if the separation wasn't bad enough "rage of achilles" went out of business just as the album was finished. The record deal was instead handed over to finnish label "firedoom" who not only released the album but also offered a deal for yet another album. This time things went more smooth and in 2008, after three years of slow progression, the album "six waves of woe" was finished. And that brings us up to now.

Six Waves Of Woe was released at the end of october. You have started to write the songs years earlier, as i know. You spent a lot of time and energie on writing. Was it your goal to create your life's biggest masterpiece?

Not really. Ofcourse I wanted to top "Departure" and perfect my formula but I wasn't aiming for the biggest masterpiece of my life. I don't think I'll ever have that aim. It's also important to know that during those three days I far from worked with the album on a daily basis. My work and social life doesn't really allow that. If I were to make the masterpiece of my life I would need to put myself in a situation where I could focus 100% on my music. Maybee I'll do that one day. Most likely not.
Darkness, melancholy, sadness, and void. Your music has it all. Please tell us something about each songs!

I assume you mean each song on "Six Waves of Woe". Here we go. First there is "Submission", a rather short song in terms of lyrics. It's a song about waking up in a relationship where pretty much everything has gone wrong and where the victim can see no other solution than to submit to the brutal reign of it's master no matter how dark and destructive that destiny may be. It's unfortunatelly a rather common tragedy that has been around for ages and doesn't really seem to vanish. The next song is "Selfdestructive" which is about trying to save your loved one out of the same destructive pattern that almost destroyed you once. It's a song about the desperation you feel when the end is closing in with it's suffocating darkness. The third song "Detached" can be interpreted as "Selfdestructive" but seen in the eyes of the other part, that is one falling into the dark. While "Selfdestructive" actually has some hope and some kind of "lets work this out" attitude "Detached" is all about selfpity, losing hope and fading away. Next song, "Moments in Solitude", moves away from the relationship theme and is more egocentric. It's actually a really old song from the first demo that has been reworked. The story here is about being suffocated by a reality you don't seem compatible with and neither want nor can understand. In short terms it's a song about a misanthropic hermit. The fifth song, "Pernicous", is about being poisoned by dreadful experiences and how these can be powerfull enough to drag you down into a life of complete darkness. It's also about the desperate attempts to reach out for light even though it's of no use. To some extent the lyrics were inspired by the stories told by people surviving brutal massacres or being witnesses of such. Finally, the last song "Deprived" is about falling victim to promises of everlasting bliss that instead turn out to be delusive tricks to capture and encage. It can be seen as both falling victim to religious beliefs or sects and being mislead into a depriving relationship.
Who helped you in the writing process? You don't have full-time members in your band, is it intentional?

Everything on the album apart from the final mastering was done by me. Doing everything by yourself has ofcourse it's pros and cons but that I do it that way is intentional and I have no plans of getting any help.

There's a Forest Of Shadows track on the Katatonia Tribute album. I think that there's some similarities between your and Katatonia'a music. What do you think about it, what are the main differences and similarities of your music compared to Katatonia?

At the time when we did the "Rainroom" cover we were very inspired by Katatonia and especially their minimalistic style. I would definatelly say that there are more differences than similarities. If there are similarities it's probably the minimalistic and repeatative structure of the songs and the use of rather simplistic pumping riffs.

Four years passed since the release of "Departure", which was an awesome record. How succesful was that record? How much succesc do you expect from the new release? By the way, do you even care about the success?

It was succesfull enough to sell all copies of the album and it gained quite good critics. Still, it wasn't succesfull enough to attract a major deal or a second pressing. No matter what I'm quite pleased with the album just as I am with "Six Waves of Woe". I have no idea if the new album will be a greater success or not and to be honest such things are more or less impossible to know. To me it's a better album in many ways and it should be more attractive for a wider audience but at the same time that might make it a less interesting. What do I know? To answer your final question I do care about success, not in a way that I'm aiming for a career but rather to reach out to as many as possible with my music.
The front cover of the CD is brilliant. We have already seen some similar type of front covers, but this one is so special. What do you think about it?

I'm very satisified with it and I would definatelly consider it the best one so far. You are right about it not being all that original but still there is as you say something that makes it a bit special and I think it fits the title of the album very well.
Firedoom Music is not a major record label, but it seems like they really do anything for their bands. Are you satisfied with them?
Very. I think it's a perfect label for a band such as "Forest of Shadows" that need to work under total freedom and not expect to release albums every year.
Will you ever play live? Do you want to spread this special melancholic atmosphere in a live show?

When we still were a band we did a couple of shows but they didn't motivate us enough to carry on with that. At the present there isn't even a band since I'm the only member so there is very little that points at any kind of possibillity for future live shows. It would be fun though but I'm not sure it would be worth the price it would cost with all preparations and all. Also, "Forest of Shadows" is a small band and it would be quite hard to attract any bigger audiences whih as I see it is a must if I were to even think about doing any gigs.
I think that a lot of people will like the new album. I wish you the best in the future!
Thanks.

item.author: David E. Gehlke
item.p: You've been doing Forest of Shadows for over a decade now. Did you imagine when you started you'd be doing this for such a long period of time?

I don't think I reflected much about the future at all when I started the band. It was too much of a project back then and I couldn't even see myself releasing a proper album or having a fanbase. I had dreams ofcourse but I didn't find them very realistic and I enjoyed what I had without aiming for the stars. Even though Forest of Shadows is still a quite small and obscure band it has by far fullfilled the visions and dreams I had a decade ago.
You personally, how have you developed from the first demo?

On a personal level I have changed quite alot and I'm far from the person I was back then. When you grow older you hear, feel, see and experience more. Altogether you gain a wider perspective on things and I guess that's what you might define as maturing. Musically this means that my expression includes more than what it did ten years ago. I have learnt that good music is more than sticking to restricting formulas and ideas. I have also learnt that looking back at your roots and staying in contact with them can be essential for the music and it's soul.
Have you become more self-aware as time has gone on? I say that because each successive FoS album brings forth new elements of maturity and insight.

Well, I like to listen to criticism no matter if it's from friends or professionalls. It can be hard to accept what people say at times but in the end it's essential for the progression of the music. Still, I wouldn't say that I streamline my music to suit other peoples taste or ideas. I just pick up what I hear people say that makes sense and try to do somehting about it. This skill or whatever is something I have gained with the years and in the beginning I wasn't as opened to critics. I had my masterplan and nothing could change it.
You experienced some label difficulties early in your career with Rage of Achilles going under, so how has your relationship with Firebox/Firedoom worked out?

It has worked out very well. I liked working with Rage of Achilles just as I do with Firebox/Firedoom but they weren't as professional. Even though I don't make music for a living I appreciate if the label give the band the attention it deserves and do everything in its power to market the music. This, total freedom and much more is what I get from Firebox/Firedoom and I'm very happy about it all.
To you, what does Six Waves of Woe represent?

Basically, six dark and depressive songs. It's not a concept album and there's no theme that runs thru all songs. The title only represent the least common divisor of the albums six songs. As with most other songs I have composed this would be the elements of sorrow, losing hope and despair. When I was thinking about putting this unification into words I had an idea of using the sea as a metaphore. I don't know why but for me the sea has always had some kind of dark aura especially the cold, wretched sea we have here up in the north. To make each song a wave that crush down on life with its cold, dark woe felt quite powerfull. I guess that sums it all up.
The cover image is striking. Is this something you shot yourself? What does it represent?

It's a picture I have taken with my crappy camera and it portraits the landscape where I grew up. It's a view I have seen a million times that bring to life alot of old memories both good and bad. What I wanted to illustrate with the picture is the cold, dark and desolate aura of the sea, that is the one the title is focused around. I would love to have a motivation for the dead tree that really completes the picture but I have none apart from that it looked good. Maybee the dead tree was subconciously needed to stick to the formula since there's always trees present on my albums.
Does it all tie into your view of nature and if so, what are some of the key points of the story?

Nature is complex. The same view can tell a thousand stories as with the view portraited on the album cover. I guess my view of nature is that it's a silent watcher. A comforter for your mental state. At least I find comfort when I move around in nature. I can think of no better way to illustrate feelings than by using pictures of nature since thoose pictures will tell a different story to each viewer just as the interpretations of the music and lyrics do.
You worked on the album for over three years - was it difficult to get everything together?

I don't find it difficult to get it all together. At least not musically. The big challenge for me is the lyrics. I don't want them to be romantic nonsense filled with empty phrases. It's ofcourse hard to know if lyrics are nonsense or not but to me and my relation to the songs it's important that they have a true meaning and some substance. Another challenge is to know your limitations and to know when the songs won't get any better. I want to do the songs as much justice as possible but my skills both when it comes to instrumentation and recording can be restricting.
What are the benefits of working alone? Drawbacks?

The benefits are total freedom both artisitically and when it comes to when I want to work or not. The drawbacks are your own limitations as composer/musican/producer. When I worked with Micce I could always rely on his skills and that he would say if something was good or not. Now, I have to be my own judge which can be hard especially if you are a perfectionsit as I am.
In relation to Departure, where does this album rank? Better? More personal?

I like to belive that every album I produce is a better version of its predecessor but I'm quite aware of that the reality is quite different. If I were to redo "Departure" as a successor to "Six Waves of Woe" it might have turned out as the better one but as I see it today it's not. Maybee for the simple reason that the sound of "Six Waves of Woe" is more up-to-date with what I like today. Maybee in ten years I won't think the same. Still, I would definatelly say that it's a more personal album though simply because it's done entirely by me. "Departure" was colored by the cooperation with Micce and as such it was not as much a personal piece of work.
The new album appears to be a bit more dark than Departure and your vocals are more dreary than ever. Was that the intended the goal? Something like "Selfdestructive" instantly comes to mind.

Ofcourse. My goal with "Forest of Shadows" is to make music that is dark and dreary and the more I learn how to express this, the closer I will get to my goal. Also, by making the songs more personal it's easier to express true emotions and true emotions are hard to compete with.
I'm really intrigued by "Moments in Solitude" both lyrically and sonically. What's the story behind this one?

It's based on a very old song, actually the first full song I ever wrote for "Forest of Shadows". Due to its age the melodies and the way the song is arranged is quite close to the original formula. Spiced up with news ways of thinking the result is quite different from any other song on the album. I kind of like it since it links to the roots of the band and makes it evident that the original formula still works. Lyrically it's a song about a hopeless attempt to escape a depressive reality in which I didn't want to belong. To some extent it's a song about being naive. It can also be seen as a song about being your own in your own world. An ode to the misanthropic hermit.
Is the Niclas in Forest of Shadows anything like the Niclas outside of the band?

Well, I claim that the music is personal so there must be some kind of resemblance. I think the big difference is that I don't express what I do with music in life, that is I don't look lie a suicidal black hole. It would be hard to pinpoint me as the person behind the band in a crowd of people. You would probably also find it hard to link me with my music if you spoke to me in private since I'm a bit more positive and, well, "oridinary". The Niclas in Forest of Shadows is a mental state I keep for myself and the music.
Do you do a good job of separating life from art?

To some extent I don't want to seperate them totally simply because art is such a big part of my life. I don't want to live a life in denial. Still, I can't live the life I portrait in my music since that would be devastating. I guess a perfect blend is what I aim for.
Where do you think the appeal lies with such dark, harrowing music?

Catharasis maybee. To make music that is based on negative things in life is quite close to going in therapy. Since most people need that every now and then it's quite appealing to have some kind of tool for fixing your own problems by yourself. Ofcourse I know that it doesn't work all the time but it's still some kind of comfort to have a ventilation for your inner feelings.
Finally, what are your plans going into 2009?

I think I will take a break and gain some distance in order to find new motivation for a new album. Since I work under total freedom and since I want to work that way I can't really say when I will start working on new songs. Maybe tomorrow, maybe in two years. All know is that there will be new songs.

item.author: Sebastien
item.p: Hello forest of shadows! What is your actually line up and where do you come from?
Forest of Shadows come from Stockholm, Sweden and is at the moment a one-man band consisting of me, Niclas Frohagen.
Your last album "departure" is a beautiful piece of metal, what are your influences?
How did the recording occur? Are you satisfies by it? Have you got echoes about the album?

The recording was very problematic. It started out as an album by me and Micce but we soon ran out of motivation since we had different visions about what it all should sound like. After a while I came to the conclusion that the only way to get it all done without causing a major catastophe was to do it all by myself. When Micce left I had to start all over since he had been in charge of both the recording and the instrumentation. It was quite a journey and I can't say I liked it all the time but it finally came to an end and considering the consequences I must say I'm pretty satisfied with the result. And it seems like the media thinks the same since most reviews have very positive. It even got full score in some zines and was rated album of the year.
About this album, what is the concept behind the music?

I'm sorry if I break the illusion but there isn't really a concept behind it. It's a collection of rather depressive songs that happened to end up as at least lyrically pretty similar. Ofcourse I did my best to unify the songs production-wise since I quite like such albums but as I said they weren't composed to fit some concept.
Which are your projects to come (after all promos of this record)? Envisaged live-shows in France or Europe?

Since I'm the only member of the band live-shows are pretty much out of the question. Instead I will focus on the upcoming album whatever that will be. I have alot of ideas and rough versions of songs that I really want to work with.
If you have a message to make pass to your metal fans, it is to you!
Check out "departure" if you're into dark and depressive music and stay updated with www.forestofshadows.com if you want to know more about the upcoming album...

item.author: Christopher McGuirk
item.p: It's nice to be introduced to truly refreshing Doom Metal every now and then; Niclas Frohagen is particularly good at that. His solo project, Forest of Shadows, now has a new album out and a comfortable deal with a Finnish label that has been an impressive addition to the metal scene for quite some time. In order to get an insight into one of Doom's brightest stars, I bit the bullet and asked if he'd care to give MWRI an interview. He agreed to do it, so sit back and enjoy an interview with one of alternative music's most eclectic stars.
First of all, congratulations on the simply superb 'Departure' LP. Do you feel the album has been warmly received?
Yeah, most of the critics has been very positive.
I notice that you play all the instruments on the LP. What prompted the decision to be a one-man band?

The last year of my cooperation with Micce Andersson wasn't all that positive and I really felt that we were drifting apart musically. As I saw it we would go seperate ways sooner or later and the idea of turning the band into a one-man band started spinning in my mind. When I moved from Gothenburg to Stockholm in the middle of the production of "departure" I realized that there was no chance that we would manage to pull it through together with both the distance and the lack of motivation. I thought that the best thing for both of us would be to split up.
Finland has proven to be a hotbed of talent these past few years. Do you feel Finnish metal is about to finally rise to prominence on the world stage?
Some Finnish bands are already in my humble opinion among the best in the world in their genre and there's alot of new fresh bands that most likely will go as far.
Your latest album is an exquisite piece of Melodic Doom metal. Have you always been a fan of this genre, or do you feel your music has developed over time to incorporate this style?

Well, since the first time I heard a band of this genre I have been quite addicted to it. There's so much more that has influenced me though so to answer your question I would say that the style has been developed over time. I actually started composing long before I knew about doom metal.
Which bands would you cite as your biggest influences, and why?

That's a difficult question. If we are looking at Forest of Shadows from the day the band was founded until today I would say Opeth, Katatonia, My Dying Bride, Anathema, Shape of Despair and Cult of Luna. Opeth inspire me with their epic and beautifull melodies and arrangements, Katatonia with their simple and powerfull riffs, My Dying Bride with their dark atmosphere, Anathema with their emotional songs, Shape of Despair with the slow, monotonous and haunting sound and finally Cult of Luna with their fresh brew of hardcore and doom.
Are you friendly with other bands on the Firedoom label? Have you found your time spent with them?
I haven't had any contact with any of them. It would have been fun to get to know some of them but I'm too lazy to take the innitiative.
Do you have plans to form a live band and tour? If so, would you consider playing in the UK for some dates?

There's no plans of putting together a live-act. We tried that once and it wasn't very successfull. I guess the songs are better suited for a home environment than the local rock club. It would be very cool to play the songs live some day with a proper line-up and at a proper place but it's more of a dream than something I plan. If it would happen then I'm pretty sure we would come to the UK.
If you could describe your sound in 5 words, how would you do it?
Depressive, dark, monotone, slow and epic.
Finally, how do you intend to follow up 'Departure'? Can we expect more of the same, or are you thinking of changing direction?

There will hopefully be something new about it but basically it will probaly be pretty much the same. It might be more electronic or more old-school. Who knows? I'll just do whatever sounds good and feels right. As long as it's dark and depressive I don't feel that I have let anyone down...

item.author: Karl Haikara
item.p: How's it going for the band?

It's going just fine. Well, there's actually not much happening right now with the band apart from some maintenance of the website. I'm giving it all a little break since the production of "departure" was so very intense and I really need to grow some distance in order to be motivated for the new material.
Give us a short history of the band, and tell us who is in it.

Forest of Shadows was founded in 1997 by me, Niclas Frohagen and was initially a solo-project. After the release of the first demo "The Silent Cry" in 1998 multi-instrumentalist Micce Andersson joined the band. Together we recorded two demos: "promotion-cd 1999" and "promotion-cd 2000" before Martin Claesson joined on guitar and the band switched from being a studio band to a live-performing act. In the summer of 2000 after two less successfull gigs with four different session/fulltime-members coming and going we dropped Martin Claesson and went back to being a studio band. The demo/mcd "where dreams turn to dust" was recorded and shipped out to different labels where "Molded Skull" was the first one to show interest and also signed us with a first release being the mcd. Unfortunatelly "Molded Skull" ended up with financial problems and instead "Rage of Achilles" showed up and took over the release. In October 2001, one and a half year after it had been recorded, the mcd was released. It was well received by the media and the expectations of the upcoming full-length were high. The creation of this full-length (departure) would turn out to be one long and painfull journey with Micce leaving the band and Rage of Achilles ending up in financial problems. About four years after the creation of the album was initialized it finally got released thru Firedoom/Firebox.
Any news you want to announce to your fans?
Right now there are no official news to share with the audience. In a couple of months there will surely be some though so keep updated with the website.
Who are your main influences musically?

Right now, the bands that influence me most are: Cult of Luna, Isis, Anekdoten, Antimatter, Anathema (The Silent Enigma and Eternity), Katatonia (new stuff), Shape of despair and The Funeral Orchestra.
What influences the lyrics?
Poetry, films, other bands lyrics and personal experiences.
What mood or feeling do you wish to impart upon the listener?

A rather dark, desperate and dismal one :) There are so many happy and positive bands out there that I see it as my quest to be their negation. Not even the bands that claim to be dark and depressive live up to it. I guess many bands see it as pretentious and overdone but I don't.
Do you think you fit the "Doom Metal" title, or how would you describe your music to some body who hasn't heard it?
There are doomy elements in the music but it wouldn't be fair to label it as doom-metal. I feel quite satisifed by labeling it as just dark music. I mean some parts aren't even metal.
(for the guitarist(s), bassist and drummer) What gear do you use?

Fender hearffield guitar, vester acoustic guitar, washburn XB-100 bass, marshall valvestate amp, behringer v-amp, levin 12-string mandolin, august hoffman piano, african djembe, shure microphones and lots of samples.
How does the band write the songs?

At the moment I write all the material. Even when there were other members involved I wrote most of the stuff. I'm not very democratic when it comes to music and a true control freak which makes me rather hard to work with.
What do you listen to, as in, if I were to look into your cd collection what would I see?

Mostly metal but also some progressive rock, trip-hop, classical, darkwave and pop. I have a rather huge collection so there's most likely something for everyone especially if you like dark music or metal.
What do you think of the following styles: A) Doom Metal, B) Black metal, C) Gothic Metal, D) Death Metal (both melodic and otherwise) and E) Power Metal

I'm not that obsessed by the actuall styles. I could name alot of bands in all these style that I really don't like. Generally, I prefer doom and black but I listen to alot of gothic and death as well. There are very few power-metal bands that interest me. The bands that I like most are either the founders of their genre or impossible to place in one.
How do you think the Swedish scene is doing, and what do you think of the bands from Norway and Finland?

Without being too much of a patriot I would say that the swedish scene is the most interesting one in the world especially considering that Sweden is a rather small country. Most bands are more known outside Sweden though so it's really hard to keep track fo the scene. Norway and Finland are also very interesting countries that deliver alot of good music. Most of the bands that I really like are from one of these three countries.
What do you think of Satanism in metal?

Depends on what you mean. To make satanism a part of the show can be both funny, cool and pathetic. It all depends on how you do it. The word satanism is actually rather tricky since it represents alot of ideas that aren't unified. The inverted-christianity kind of satanism that is about death and destruction is in my opinion as fucked up as chistianity or any other big religion. Being a rather hardcore atheist I find all supernatural beliefs as unattractive and stupid and I don't any of them to be synonymous to metal.
One thing I like is your artwork, who does it, and do you think it imparts the feeling of your music?
I do it and yes it's supposed to visualize of the mood I create with my music.
Does the natural enviroment of Sweden or of nature in general inspire you in any way?
It's rather inspiring to walk around in nature but I don't think the swedish nature as such inspires me more than any other northern nature.
What do you think of the following statement: "Death is a natural thing, there is no reason to fear it"?

Since I don't belive in a life after death or at least don't see it as a very likely scenario I do fear death in a sense that I don't feel ready to die. Still I know that it's a natural part of life and there not much to do about it.
Does the atmosphere of a funeral inspire you (nice joke question here)?

I haven't attended that many funerals and I never felt more or less inspired at any of them. Ofcourse the death of a close one can inspire or at least you might need music to be able to deal with emotions that are hard to express in other ways.
How are gigs going, what kind of reception do you get?
Since I'm the only memeber gigs are pretty much out of the question.
What are your plans for the near future?
To start writing new material. I have alot of ideas already and I just do something about them. I might record something within a couple of months.
Finally, is there anything you'd like to say to the readers of Tmetal?
Give "departure" a chance and hopefully you'll discover something new and if not then there's not much I can do about it...

item.author: Christian
item.p: First of all, gratulation for your debut album "Departure". It's a masterpiece of atmospheric and melodic Doom Metal. Are you also satisfied with the album?

Yeah, I'm quite satisifed with it. There are ofcourse technical details that could be improved quite alot but considering the conditions under which I was producing the album I have to accept that I can't compete with proffesional super productions.
You are quasi a one-man-band? Are there more advantages or disadvantages?

Considering my current situation with a quite demanding work in the gaming industry I don't get much time to spend on my music on a daily basis and it would be hell to try and cooperate with someone else. I also feel more comfortable when I have full control of the process and when things are done my way. Forest of Shadows are too important to me to allow for compromizes. So, generally speaking the advantages are more than the disadvantages.
After you have split with Micce Anderson, who was also involved in writing the songs, what have been the biggest problems?

To replace Micce's skill as an instrumentalist. My role in the band while Micce still was a member was more or less only to write the music and lyrics. I did some rythm guitars and programming and ofcourse all the vocals but apart from that Micce did everything. He was also in charge of the recordings and owned the fancy gear such as mixers, effects and amplifiers.
Is it necessary, to be in a special mood for wirting such songs of pure sadness?

It's not necessary but it helps. At least to come up with the basic ideas and melodies. The rest can be done no matter what state I'm in. I usually have a pretty clear idea of what it all should sound like in terms of production and that idea is always there.
On "Departure" the sound of the guitars is very dominant - in some cases even the vocals seem to disappear into the guitarsound. Was this your intention?

Are they? That sure wasn't my intention. Or actually it depends on what you mean. I didn't want them to drown the vocals but I wanted them to pretty dominant apart from that. I didn't want it to sound too symphonic.
The most tracks on "Departure" are quite long. Do your songs need this time, to develop their whole potential - or do you just have a soft spot, to create songs beyond ten minutes?

I guess the songs just end up as very long. When I come up with a melody or harmony that I really like I want to give it the time it deserves. The alternative would be to split up the songs and instead have alot of songs that sound pretty similar. I really don't see the point in doing so. Working with slow music also makes the songs last for ages. I used to be a member in a thrash metal band were the music was like four times as fast, at least. When I wrote lyrics for these songs I ended up with texts that were as long as the ones I wrote for "forest of shadows". I mean if you take an arbitrary thrash song and play it at a fourth of the tempo it will last for over ten minutes.
Reading the lyrics, I have found many places, where you tell about the transience of beauty and love. What do you want to express with your words?

They don't have any hidden messages if that's what you're wondering. They're just stories. Rather sad ones. I guess I just want the lyrics to fit the mood of the music. It just wouldn't work to use political or gore lyrics with the music of "forest of shadows". There's alot political and philosophical things I would like to express and I used to do that in the thrash/death band "shubend" in which I used to be a member.
Nearly each track on "Departure" got rewritten several times - so that the album took three years, to become "real". Are you such a perfectionist?
Yes. Actually, it was not only a matter of perfectionism but also about turning the album into what I wanted but didn't get when I worked with Micce.
I could really imagine, that your songs would sound very cool at a live gig. But as you are "alone", this remains probably only imagination. Am I wright?

Most likely. I mean I could probably get some session members. After all the songs are pretty simple to play. The big problem would be to arrange the songs in order to work in a "live" situation. It would also need a certain environment. Playing the songs at the local pub with alot of drunk people in the crowd and a horrible sound production wouldn't be fair to the songs. I have seen to many gigs with that happening.

You are a kind of a jack-of-all-trades. You played on "Departure" all instruments, wrote the lyrics, did the recording and mixing and also designed the booklet. Additionally you design and update your homepage on your own. Is there anything, you can't do?

Relax? Well, to be honest there's alot of things I can't do and there is much to wish for when it comes to my instrumental skills. Still, I like the idea of doing it all by myself no matter if it could be way better if I hired session musicans. It gets more "true" and personal and uniform.
Do you plan to take additional band members into FOREST OF SHADOWS in the near future?
I don't plan to but I wouldn't say no if the perfect companion showed up.
Besides FOREST OF SHADOWS, you are/were also part of some side-projects. Could you please tell us more about these?

The two main projects/bands are Ningizzia and Shubend. Ninigizzia is band I have with Stephane Peudpin in France. Our music isn't all too different from Forest of Shadows and I would guess that we share the same types of listeners. An album to recommend is the full-length "dolorous novella" which we release some year ago. It's a really dark and rather diverse album. Shubend, which I'm no longer in, is on the other hand more of a thrash/death band. I wouldn't guess that we share many listeners at all but still it might be interesting to check them out. We released a full-length on Rage of Achilles some year ago called "Synergism" and we recorded an album rather recently called "Posthumous" which unfortunatelly never got a proper release. Most likely we will share the songs as mp3's instead of letting them rot in obsurity.
What kind of music is at the time in your CD-player?

I just listend to soilwork, a band that hasn't picked up my attention before, and I must say they're really good. Not the typical music I listen to these days though. The better bands that spin in my cd-player these days are bands like cult of luna, isis, funeral orchestra and shape of depair.
Besides your love and half-time profession of making music, you have a job in the computer games industry. Can you tell us more about that?

Well, I work with sound/music as a programmer at a company called Avalanche Studios here in Stockholm. It's quite fun even though it has a rather negative impact on my music not to mention my social life since it demands quite alot of time. What makes me pay this rather high price is that it feels much more rock'n'roll to work games than to work at some boring company with boring tasks. I'm not saying that all companies outside the gaming industry are boring and unattractive but unfortunatelly most of them.
What are your musical plans for the upcomming months?
To continue to grow some distance from "departure" so I can to start my next project with new fresh ideas instead of just repeating what I did on that album.

item.author: Josean
item.p: The first thing that calls my attention is that Forest Of Shadows is a one man band. How do you manage to record by yourself all the instruments?

It wasn't easy, even though the songs are far from technical. I mean I'm not really an instrumentalist in any way. I know how to handle the guitar and piano but at a rather basic level. Anyways, I had a will of doing it and given enough time I knew I would pull it through in some way. And so I did. I guess it helped that I arranged the music in such a way to make it as easy as possible to play without losing any of the musical quality.
Don't you have the help of any session musician?

Not on "departure". I guess somewhere deep inside I really wanted to do it all by myself. I could have asked some of the musical friends that I know to help me out but I didn't. Maybe I'll do that for the next release. Or maybe I won't. There are rather positive effects of doing it all by yourself. The album gets way more personal and it's easier to control the final result. And since I'm a control freak when it comes to music I really value that.
Has it always been like this or was ever there more band members?

There have been a lot of members coming and going. Micce Andersson is one of these and the one that stayed in the band for longest time. He had a key role in forming Forest of Shadows into what it has become in terms of sound and arrangements. He was also something of a mentor to me and I learnt alot from him about music in general.
What musical studies have you? What instrument do you identify yourself with the most?

The only musical education I have apart from piano lessons when I was a kid is a survey course in how music is used in film. So pretty much none I would say. I'm pretty much self learnt which has its pros and cons. I have actually always been more into composing than playing. I started composing music on the guitar more or less the same day I learnt to play it. My main instrument is probably piano since it's the one I feel most comfortable improvising on.
For a single person, composing an album and recording must be tough work. Is this the reason why it's taken you three years to release your sophomore album or are there other reasons?

That and the fact that I am a normal person with a normal work that takes up a lot of my time. Well, actually I wasn't employed during the entire process so I can't just blame it on my work. I guess a more accurate reason why it took so long is the fact that I'm a perfectionist. I didn't care about the restrictions set by my skills at performing, recording and most important of all my studio equipment. I still wanted it to sound as good or even better as an average, professionally produced metal album.
The tracks were composed between the 2000-2002 period, when you entered the studio to record, did you make some further arragements there or did they remain such as they were?

Well, to start with I never entered an actual studio since I recorded everything in my apartment. Anyways, I did change the arrangements quite a lot during the recording process. This is quite evident if you take the oldest song - "November dream" - and compare it with the original recording on the demo "The Silent Cry" from 1998.
Have you already composed tracks for your next album?

I have some sketches and ideas but nothing that is near a final composition. The thing is that I haven't even signed a deal for my next album. There will be a next album though but I'm not sure when I will start working on it seriously. I need to give it all some distance since the years with "departure" had quite a negative impact on my health and life in general. I lived and breathed that album for like three years and it felt like being haunted.

Apart from composing and playing all the instruments, you have also recorded and mixed the tracks. Don't you trust anyone or is it a more comfortable way of working and also a way of saving money in the production process?

It was mostly because of money but it's also a comfortable way of working since I can record whenever I feel motivated and inspired. I wouldn't say it's lack of trust that is the big problem. More that I see the recording and mixing as a very important part of the artwork and something I want to control to 100%. I don't think there are many producers that want to work under such conditions.
You mix Doom or Funeral Doom with certain Swedish Folk airs. Where does your inspiration come from?

When it comes to doom I would say that I'm more influenced by the style in general than specific bands. In my opinion there are very few good doom bands but the ones that are good are really good. For some reason most doom bands sound dull to me. They have alot of the classical elements but there is always something missing.

Swedish folk music is something I started to enjoy when I teamed up with Micce in 1998. My interest for this music has grown ever since. I can't say that I like everything that is labeled "Swedish Folk". But I mean that's just like with all other styles. There's lots of crappy metal music for instance. There is something genuine and original in folk music that is hard to find anywhere else and I guess that is what inspires me most.
The electronic intro might even remind people of more alternative bands such as Portishead or Massive Attack. Have these bands and their sound influenced you?

I listen to the mentioned bands every now and then and I like them a lot. What these bands have learnt me is that it's more than possible to make doomy music without distored guitars and big drums. I guess there is doom and there is doom and to me it's all the same. I care more about what lies beneath surface than the surface itself.
Apart from Forest Of Shadows. Do you play in other bands?

I am one of two members of the band Ningizzia and up until recently I did the vocals in a band called Shubend. The music of Ningizzia is quite similar to Forest of Shadows and I would really recommend it. Shubend on the other hand is more of a technical thrash/death band and I wouldn't bet too much on that the people who like this band likes Forest of Shadows.
You come from Sweden and have found for the Finish label Firedoom. How did you get in contact with this label?

I got a tip from Duncan at Rage of Achilles (the label that initially was supposed to release "departure") to contact them. Actually Duncan helped me out to establish the contact and I'm very thankful for that.
I guess that being a single-man band, playing live it's quite difficult or impossible. How do you see the chances of playing live?

I actually don't see them at all. Not only would it require a lot of session musicans but also some major rearrangements of the songs to make them work live. After all Forest of Shadows is a studio band and it's hard or even impossible to create the same atmosphere live. Still, I do dream every now and then of playing live with Forest of Shadows. And who knows? It has happened before so it might happen again.

item.author: gero
item.p: I think "Departure" in one of the best album of the past year. After four years of work, are you satisfied with the result?

Considering the fact that it was all done by myself with a more or less non-existing budget I must say that I am very satisfied. On the other hand I am very aware of how much better it could have been if it would have been recorded in a "real" studio with a nice producer. Still, what counts isn't the polished surface but what lies beneath, which is something that I'm very proud of.
How Micce Andersson contributed to the composition process? Why did he leave the band shortly before the release of the album?

He co-wrote most of the stuff and we almost pulled it all through together. It was in the middle of first recording session when I realized how very different our visions were about the final result and I just couldn't see myself being part of an album built on compromizes. So, no matter how important Micce was to the band I just felt that I had to finish it all by myself.
You had many problems with some labels, but I think you have found in Firedoom the best company according to your style and mood... do you agree?

Well, even though Rage of Achilles, the previous label, was a very good and suitable label for the band I must say that Firedoom is even better. I don't know if Firedoom is the ultimate label but they have done a very good job so far.
What is the "Forest Of Shadows"? And from what is your "Departure"?

Depends on who you ask :) If you ask someone who is into dungeons and dragons they would say that it's a place in their fantasy world. When I came up with the name I didn't know anything about this so there's no connection whatsoever between the band and roleplaying. There were also plans about making a Harry Potter book called "Harry Potter and the Forest of Shadows". I don't know what happened with that and I sure didn't have anything to do with it. Seriously. So, what is my "Forest of Shadows" then? Well, a description of a pretty dark and pretty lifeless reality. That's what I wanted it to symbolize anyway.

The title "departure" can be interpreted in many ways. All the songs are about leaving life either perceived by the one leaving or the one left behind. So that's one interpretation. Another more personal interpretation is that symbolize what was happening during the time which the album was recorded. During this time I started a new life and left an old one behind I also moved into a new direction musically and left the more traditional metal scene behind. Micce, the most important member of the band, apart from me :), also departed from the band. So to conclude the title "departure" felt pretty suitable.
What are the feelings you want to express with your music? It's dark, but in certain moments it seems to open for some hope...

It's pretty hard to put down in words exactly what I want to express with my music. I want to create an overall dark and sinister but still in some way beautifull atmosphere. I guess hope is what makes it beautifull, that is if there is any hope in my music.
Recently I have listened to another band that plays music similar to yours... it's the dutch band Deinonychus, have you ever got in touch with them?

I own one album with Deinonychus called "The Weeping of a Thousand Years". Can't say it's my favourite album but it's not all that bad. Apart from that I haven't heard much but maybee I should. I know that mr Deinonychus plays (or played) in Bethlehem which is one of my favourite bands. At least on "Dark Metal" and "Dictius te Necare". I wouldn't say that they sound like "Forest of Shadows" though. I have never spoken with any member of either Deinonychus or Bethlehem.
What are your main influences? And your favourite listens at the moment?

My most recent influences are: Cult of Luna, Isis, Anekdoten, Shape of Despair, Katatonia and Antimater. There are some other as well but they aren't as important. Some of the favourite bands that I listen to these days are: The above mentioned as well as Garmarna, Darkthrone, Satyricon, The Provenance, Paatos, The Funeral Orchestra, Zyklon, Napalm Death and Rapture.
What are the advantages and disadvantages of having a real band versus playing completely alone?

If you're involved in a real band you have to invest quite alot of time in it on a regular basis and that's not something I am able to do right now. In the long run it's way more time consuming to do it all by yourself but at least I get to choose when I am to spend my time. It's less frustrating on a creative level to be solo since you don't to compromize and stuff but it's always more fun to be able to share the joy of creating something good with other band members. If I would have had the time and money to be able to run the band as a fulltime work then I would definatelly team up with some good people but that's not the case here.
Obviously we're not going to see you in tour...?! Do you think your music is suitable for live shows?

Not as a rock'n'roll show. It might work pretty well if it was performed in a more dramatic and magic scenery like some old theater or something like that. It would also demand quite alot of musicans and alot of rearranging of the songs since they aren't made for the standard setup.
Keep up the good works, for "Departure" is truly one of them!
Thanx

item.author: Robert Hoyem
item.p: Death / doom blandet med endel elektronikk. Ikke akkurat dagligdags kost. Det er iallefall hva Forest of Shadows driver med, og de (han) gjÃ¸r det pÃ¥ en helt utmerket mÃ¥te. Hvis du ikke har skaffet deg et av fjorÃ¥rets beste doom-plater sÃ¥ er det pÃ¥ tide Ã¥ gjÃ¸re det nÃ¥. Jeg har uansett sendt avgÃ¥rde noen elektroniske spÃ¸rsmÃ¥l til bandets eneste medlem, Niclas Frohagen, og fikk svar pÃ¥ dem ganske nylig...
Heisann - gratulerer med "Departure", og takk for et av Ã¥rets beste plater!
Det gleder meg Ã¥ hÃ¸re at du anser "Departure" som et av Ã¥rets beste plater.
For det har ikke vÃ¦rt bare enkelt Ã¥ fÃ¥ debutplata ut i butikken. Den skulle vel egentlig ut pÃ¥ Rage of Achilles, men sÃ¥nn gikk det ikke til?

Rage of Achilles skulle egentlig gi ut skiva, men det ble avlyst helt mot slutten pÃ¥ grunn av Ã¸konomiske problemer. Endog hjelpte de til med Ã¥ finne et nytt plateselskap (Firebox / Firedoom) som kunne ta over plateslippet, sÃ¥ det hele gikk ganske bra til. Det var egentlig synd at det gikk som det gikk med Rage of Achilles, for de stod bak en hel del interessante skiveslipp og en masse nytenkning innen metal-sjangeren som virkelig behÃ¸ves.
Men dere slapp altsÃ¥ ei miniskive pÃ¥ Rage of Achilles. Hvordan stÃ¥r den seg i dag, i forhold til "Departure"?

Miniskiva "Where Dreams Turn To Dust" har en hel del likheter med "Departure" av den enkle grunn av at vi ville utvikle oss videre i samme retning. En del lÃ¥ter pÃ¥ "Departure" ble dessuten pÃ¥begynt samtidig som vi holdt pÃ¥ Ã¥ gjÃ¸re ferdig miniskiva. Den store forskjellen er at lydbildet er at lydbildet har fÃ¥tt mer framtredende elektroniske innslag, og at arrangementene er mer varierte og velpolerte. Jeg tror definitivt at de som liker miniskiva kommer til Ã¥ like "Departure" og visa versa.

Noe som slo meg nÃ¥r jeg kikket igjennom diskografien pÃ¥ hjemmesida til Forest of Shadows (www.forestofshadows.com), var at lÃ¥ta "November Dream" stammer sÃ¥ langt tilbake som til "The Silent Cry"-demoen fra 1998 - korriger meg gjerne om jeg tar feil. Hvordan har denne lÃ¥ta utviklet seg, og hvorfor har du valgt Ã¥ beholde den pÃ¥ full-lengderen?

Det er en veldig stor forskjell pÃ¥ hvordan "November Dream" pÃ¥ 98'-demoen og pÃ¥ "Departure". En hel del partier er skrevet pÃ¥ nytt, og en del av de gamle er strÃ¸ket. Hvorfor jeg valgte Ã¥ inkludere pÃ¥ "Departure", er av den enkle anledning at jeg synes det er ei bra lÃ¥t som ikke kom til sin rett pÃ¥ demoen. Sannsynligvis kommer lÃ¥ta "Moments of Solitude" som ogsÃ¥ finnes pÃ¥ 98'-demoen til Ã¥ bli pusset opp pÃ¥ samme mÃ¥te. Alle lÃ¥ter jeg har sluppet med Forest of Shadows er lÃ¥ter som jeg har et dyptplÃ¸yende forhold til av ulike grunner, og jeg vil derfor at alle skal fÃ¥ et bedre ytre enn hva de fikk pÃ¥ en obskur demo.

Selv om Forest of Shadows i all hovedsak for det meste har vÃ¦rt ditt prosjekt, har du hatt med noen medlemmer inni mellom - Micce blant annet er kreditert pÃ¥ endel av lÃ¥tene pÃ¥ "Departure". Hvor mye har han betydd for hvordan alt lÃ¥ter der?

Micce har betydd veldig mye for Forest of Shadows, ikke bare som medkompositÃ¸r pÃ¥ "Departure". Til forskjell fra meg er Micce en skolert musiker og han stilte derfor meget hÃ¸yere krav til bÃ¥de produksjon og lÃ¥tskrivning. Takket vÃ¦re dette styrte han bandet frÃ¥ Ã¥ vÃ¦re et obskyrt prosjekt til et band som til slutt fikk en platekontrakt og som har flere beundrere over hele verden. Utenom Micce finnes det enda et betydningsfult medlem, nemelig Martin Claessib som krediteres pÃ¥ en del av materialet pÃ¥ miniskiva - bÃ¥de som lÃ¥tskriver og tekstforfatter. Martin var akkurat som meg, ingen en supermusiker, men mer en person som brenner for sjangeren og atmosfÃ¦ren - den delen er ogsÃ¥ veldig viktig.
Hvorfor er nÃ¥ Forest of Shadows et soloprosjekt igjen?

Micce og jeg hadde ikke det samme synet pÃ¥ hvordan ting skulle lÃ¥te, og til tross hans enorme kompetanse var det mot slutten ikke holdbart Ã¥ fortsette samarbeidet. Jeg vil ganske enkelt ikke at den tida og engergien jeg legger ned i Forest of shadows skal brukes til Ã¥ lage musikk som bygger pÃ¥ kompromisser. Det holder ikke i lengden. Demokrati er ikke alltid den beste metoden.

Forest of Shadows framstÃ¥r pÃ¥ "Departure" som litt halv-elektronisk. Jeg synes faktisk jeg hÃ¸rer endel Massive Attack i musikken. Henter du pÃ¥virkninger fra andre elektroniske band, isÃ¥fall hvilke?

Massive Attack, Portishead, Garmarna, Earthling, Depeche Mode, BjÃ¶rk og Animatter er noen av de banda med elektroniske innslag som jeg inspireres av. Ã tilfÃ¸re musikken elektroniske innslag er for meg veldig viktig, ettersom det fresher opp det utslitte og veldig konservative lydbildet som finnes i de fleste metalband. Kunsten ligge i Ã¥ gjÃ¸re det pÃ¥ rett vis, slik at atmosfÃ¦ren forblir intakt. Man kan med enkelhet Ã¸delegge bra lÃ¥ter med Ã¥ modernisere dem pÃ¥ feil vis og fÃ¥ dem til Ã¥ lÃ¥te som radiopop. Band som Animatter, Massive Attack,Garmarna og Portishead har lyktes i Ã¥ lage lÃ¥ter som er mÃ¸rkere og med doomy enn mange doom-band.
PÃ¥ den mÃ¥ten lÃ¥ter liksom Forest of Shadows litt annerledes. Ikke for det, deathmetal-delen av musikken er absolutt fortreffelig ogsÃ¥. Hvilke metalband har du hentet inspirasjon fra?

Oj, det er mange. Om vil holder oss til "Departure", sÃ¥ er de viktigste inspirasjonskildene: Shape of Despair, Cult of Luna, Isis, Anathema, Opeth, Katatonia og Rapture. Spesielt de to fÃ¸rstnevnte har vÃ¦rt veldig viktig for "Departure", av den enkle grunn at begge to har vallgt Ã¥ gjÃ¸re doom metal pÃ¥ sin egen mÃ¥te, og fÃ¥tt det til veldig bra. Hva ren death-metal angÃ¥r, sÃ¥ finnes det ingen band som har inspirert meg sÃ¦rlig mye, selv om jeg hÃ¸rer veldig mye pÃ¥ band i den sjangeren.
Lyrikken pÃ¥ "Departure" er overvelmende trist, og virker veldig ektefÃ¸lt. Ligger det mye sÃ¸rgelig bak tekstene, eller er det bare historier og skildringer?

Sett utenifra, sÃ¥ er det bare historier og skildringer, men bak disse finnes det i de fleste tilfellene en for av ekte hendelse som ligger til grunn. Disse hendelsene er Ã¥penbart ikke de gladeste i mitt liv.
Trommene, de er programerte - ikke at det gjÃ¸r noe, for de er programert veldig bra, men hvorfor er de det? Hvorfor ikke en ekte trommis?

Vi spilte faktisk inn ekte trommer pÃ¥ "Departure", men resultatet ble ikke helt hva vi hÃ¥pet pÃ¥, det er selvfÃ¸lgelig trivlig med ekte ekte trommer, ettersom det gjÃ¸r lÃ¥tene mer levende, men det blir dumt Ã¥ ha det med bare for sakens skyld. Spesielt nÃ¥r man ikke er et live-band. Jeg kommer alltid til Ã¥ forsÃ¸ke Ã¥ bruke ekte trommer, men ikke til hvilken som helst pris.

Personlig sliter jeg veldig med Ã¥ plukke ut favorittpartier eller lÃ¥ter fra albumet, men om det er et parti som gir meg ekstra gÃ¥shud, sÃ¥ er det helt pÃ¥ slutten av tittelsporet, akkurat nÃ¥r du kjÃ¸rer pÃ¥ med death-vokal og hurtige trommer. Herlig. Ellers synes jeg hver av lÃ¥tene har sitt utrykk, og fungerer veldig bra pÃ¥ hver sin mÃ¥te. Har du kanskje noen egne favorittlÃ¥ter fra plata?

SelvfÃ¸lgelig. Jeg vet av mange artister som ikke kan hÃ¸re pÃ¥ sine egne saker, men personlig sÃ¥ gÃ¸r jeg det ganske ofte, dog aldri som bakgrunnsmusikk. Ved Ã¥ hÃ¸re pÃ¥ min egen musikk kan jeg forflytte meg bakover i tid til de fÃ¸lelsene jeg hadde da skiva ble spilt inn. Om man ser pÃ¥ helheten sÃ¥ synes jeg nok at tittelsporet "Departure" er det beste pÃ¥ plata. Om man i stedet fÃ¥r velge ut deler av hver lÃ¥t, sÃ¥ synes jeg nok at starten pÃ¥ "Sleeping Death", midtpartiet pÃ¥ "November Dream", slutten pÃ¥ "Bleak Dormintion", siste refrenget og framover pÃ¥ "Open Wound" og midtpartiet pÃ¥ "Departure" er hÃ¸ydepunktene.
Er du interessert i hva som foregÃ¥r i doom-metal-scenen i dag? Er det noen band som interesserer deg mer en andre?

Jeg er vel interessert, men har ikke nok til og ork til Ã¥ sette meg ned og holde meg informert. De siste bandene jeg sjekket ut og virkelig liker er Shape of Despair, Isis, Krux, Funeral Orchestra og Slumber. Noen av dem er kanskje ikke helt "doom", men det var nÃ¥ uansett de jeg kom pÃ¥...
Er du involvert i flere band? I sÃ¥fall, kan du fortelle litt om disse ogsÃ¥?

Ningizzia er et band jeg har med Stephane Peudupin som bor i Paris. Vi har hatt dette bandet sammen siden 1997, og har sluppet en demo; "The Dark Path" og en full-lengder "Dolorus Novella", samt vÃ¦rt med pÃ¥ samleskiva "The Cold, The Silent" fra Dragonflight Records, og en hyllest til Mayhem med en cover av "Freezing Moon". Musikken til Ningizzia er ganske lik Forest of Shadows med mer variasjon i bÃ¥de steming og arrangering.

Shubend er et band jeg er med i sammen med Dan Lundberg og Martin Eriksson. Vi har spilt inn to demoer "Hook, Line and Sinker" og "Singularis", samt en full-lengder "Synergism". Vi har til og med vÃ¦rt med pÃ¥ samleskiva pÃ¥ Rage of Achilles, som heter "Conquest of the Empire", som forÃ¸vrig ogsÃ¥ inneholder ei lÃ¥t fra Forest of Shadows. Likhetene mellom Shubend og Forest of Shadows er veldig fÃ¥, den stÃ¸rste likheten er nok at det er jeg som stÃ¥r bak mikrofonen i begge banda. Liker man teknisk death/thrash er det nok verdt Ã¥ sjekke ut.
Helt her til slutt mÃ¥ jeg takke for intervjuet og oppfordre deg til Ã¥ fortsette Ã¥ lage sÃ¥ fortreffelig musikk vi fÃ¥r hÃ¸re pÃ¥ "Departure"!
Selv takk. Jeg skal gjÃ¸re mitt beste for Ã¥ tilfredstille de som liker mine tidligere verk.

item.author: Haiko
item.p: Hi Niclas. Puh, after four years of hard work the record is finally finished. Very facilitated I guess?

Yeah, it was a very demanding period with respect to both time and energy so when it all was finished it felt like a great relief. When you work on something for such a long time and with such intensity you get addicted to it in a weird way. I was so used to listening to the stuff on a regular basis that I continued doing so even after the release. Not because I felt like it but more like an addiction.
What were the problems during those years? I heard about band members coming and going, but finally "Forest Of Shadows" is just you..

Well, there was just one more member apart from me that was involved in "departure" so it wasn't that much about people coming and going, more about one very important member that left the band. This member, Micce Andersson, was the main instrumentalist so his departure had quite an impact on the recording of "departure". This problem was something I felt I had to live with though since I didn't believe in us two finishing the album since we disagreed on too many details.
How is the resonance to "Departure"? I for my part must say that I really like it.
I would say it's pretty good. There has been some negative words about it but generally speaking it has been very positive. Much more than expected.
"Departure" offers very intense and very melancholic music and lyrics. How much is this atmosphere part of you? Do you have to be in a certain mood for the creation of your art?

I wouldn't label myself as melancholic, not even sad. Still, there is a huge part of me that is far from happy and I guess we all have that part inside us. Some just choose to hide it deep within. So, to answer your question I would say no, I don't have to be in a certain mood to create my music even though it help if I'm feeling a bit down.
Can you say something about your way of songwriting? What inspires you to create certain music or lyrics?

I usually base my music on a simple melody or harmony. On top of this I add layer after layer until I can't come up with anything more to add. After that I try to remove everything that I find redundant in order to make it sound less messy. Somewhere during this phase I start adding the vocals. The lyrics for the vocals are written and re-written during the entire process.

What inspires me then? Well, I get inspired by a lot of things. For music I guess other pieces of music are the main inspiration but I could get inspired by certain events in life as well. Sometime the melodies just come to you and sometimes you have to search by listening to other pieces of music.
What kind of music is Niclas Frohagen listening in private?

I try to listen to all kinds of music and focus on what's good or bad music instead of what belongs to certain genres. Currently I'm quite into stuff like Cult of Luna, Isis, Darkthrone and Shape of Despair.

How do you earn a living? I can't imagine it's possible to earn to much money with the music you create, especially with that work load, ha ha. I heard the welfare state is going down the tubes in Sweden, similarly to Germany...

I earn my living as a programmer at a game developer here in Stockholm. I work alot with sound and music so I like it very much. And, no. You can't make a living by producing my kind of music. Not the way I want it anyway. We have a welfare system in Sweden as you said that do allow people to more or less work with music on a full-time basis. This provided that you fool the system to some extent. It's not very good for the country at least not when the amount of lazy people are growing as much as it does. I wouldn't say that the welfare system is going down the tubes though but it's not very far from doing so.
Let's go back in time a little bit. Can you tell the readers something about the history of "Forest Of Shadows"? What are your aims with this band?

Well, it started out in 1997 as a solo-project of mine in that half a year later resulted in the first demo "The Silent Cry". Later on Micce Andersson joined the band and took it to a new level which was proven on the albums "promo '99" and "promo 2000". During this time Martin Claesson joined as well and the band turned into a rehearsing band with ambitions of doing live gigs. That didn't last very long though partly because of the two less good gigs we did during that time. So, some time later Martin left and me and Micce started recording what would result in our first official release, that is the mCD "where dreams turn to dust". There was some mess about who was supposed to release the demo and for some time we didn't even know if it was to be released officially at all. At last it ended up in the hands of the english label "Rage of Achilles" who released it in October 2001. The contract with RoA included a full-length as well which we started working on right before the release of the mCD. This process didn't go all that well though and lasted for quite sometime duing which Micce left the band and RoA ceased to exist due to financial problems. As we all know there was a happy end and in 2004 finnish label Firedoom released the first full-length "departure".
What is it that people in Sweden are often such great multi- instrumentalists? You for example have played any instrument On "Departure"..

It's true that I play all instruments on "departure" but there is alot of work and will behind it. Why we have so many good instrumentalists in Sweden is probably because we have a good musical education in school. Most people in Sweden play or have played some kind of instrument.
What are the plans for the future? Is "Forest Of Shadows" going to stay as a solo - project of yours? If yes, are there any plans of performing live, maybe along with session musicians?

I really don't know about the future of Forest of Shadows right now apart from that it will exist for yet a couple of albums. I have no ambition of recruiting any memebers but I wouldn't say no if someone shows up that really suits the band. There are no plans or ambitions for that matter of doing any performances with or without session musicans.
How many years will we have to wait for the next Record?
That's very hard to say. It all depends on how much time I get to spend on my music and as it is right now I wouldn't expect anything within a year from now.
Niclas, thanks for your time, much luck for the future of "Forest Of Shadows", time for some last words:
Thanx for a nice interview and good luck with the zine.

item.author: Jens
item.p: Hello Niclas, how are you doing?
I'm doing just fine, thanx.
First up, congratulations for your new album - it's really awesome. I rated it 9/10. How has the other feedback been?

Thanks alot. It's always flattering to get such high rating no matter if it's common or not. So far I have actually scored pretty well in most reviews, especially the german ones. Not everyone likes the album though and especially here in Sweden there are some critics that really have issues with it. And to some extent I understand them since it's a quite provocative album with it's monumental and monotone songs. It's very easy to label it as pretentious and overdone, even though I wouldn't consider it as such. Well pretentious in a good way maybe. I mean I really try to make it as good as possible and I don't see anything wrong with that.
What took you that long to finish the songs? The MCD "Where Dreams Turn To Dust" is now three years old.

Well, alot of things happened during these three years so it's actually not that much of a mystery why it took so long. When Micce left the band we had already been working on the new album for about a year. The material was more or less finished but it was filled with compromizes caused by our diverging visions. When I suddenly had it all in my own hands I felt that I had to straighten it out before I could continue working with it.
