I did some major rearranging which took quite alot of time and while I was doing this I also had to learn how to play it since Micce was the one who normally handled the instruments. So with this in mind it didn't take that long to produce the album.
Why didn't you write really new material? I mean the songs were all written a long time ago.

All songs apart from "November Dream" were written after the material on the mCD so it's as new as can be. Ofcourse I could have dumped the songs when Micce left and started working on new songs. But I didn't. And the reason is quite simple: I liked the songs very much and dumping them was not an option.
Considering the fact that some of the songs are from the year 1997 - would you speak of progress when comparing the MCD and the new album? I think the My Dying Bride influence is little less.

I never saw and I still can't see the resemblance to My Dying Bride in Forest of Shadows. I might be blind but I just don't think that we sound that similar, at all. Don't get me wrong, I do love My Dying Bride and they are a big influence, especially the albums "turn loose the swans" and "the angel and the dark river" but I never tried to be a My Dying Bride clone in any way. Anyways, there has definatelly been a huge amount of progress since mCD. The sound has changed from being more standard doom/death to a weird fusion of electronica, metal and rock with both more aggression and cheesy elements. On a basic level it's pretty much the same as on the mCD though.
Why did you reduce the folk elements?

Did I? I think the new album has as much folk elements as the mCD. The big difference is that there is no flute or violin on the new album. No matter what it was not my purpose to get rid of these elements. I really love folk music and I wish that I could incorporate it more into the songs.
Your former bandmate Micce Anderson has co-written nearly all of the songs. Did that happen when he was till in the band or have you worked together after the official "split"?

It happened before we split up. As I said before the material was more or less finished when he left so why it took yet another two years before it was really finished was because I rearranged it all to match my visions. If we would have continued to work together we would probably have finished the album within a couple of months.
You have recorded the album on your own. Please describe what is like to do everything? Isn't it too much work?

To do everything by yourself is very good since the album gets very personal. It also gets closer to your visions which is very satisfying. The bad thing about it is that it's very time and energy consuming. The fact that it's hard to master all instruments can also restrict the album and in my case I would say that it did. If it's too much work to do it all on your own is hard to say. If you know that the result will be better then it's definatelly worth the extra work. In my case I did it all by myself because since there was no alternative.
Was it a special challenge writing an instrumental like "Bleak Dormition" - or was it ordinary songwriting without adding some lyrics?

I wouldn't say writing an instrumental is more difficult than one with lyrics. Rather the opposite. In most of the songs that I have written I added the lyrics after the instrumental part was finished. So with that in mind, writing "Bleak Dormition" wasn't any different than any other songs apart from the fact that I didin't have to spend any time on writing lyrics for it.
The lyrics once again deal with death. Is it just because it fits to the depressive music or do they really reveal what you are thinking?

I'm not suicidal and thus they don't reveal my exact thoughts. The lyrics can be read in more than one way though and in another interpretation they could be more close what I am thinking. Why they are as they are is like you say, they fit the music. It's as simple as that.

"Departure" is released on Firedoom Music - why aren't you signed to Rage of Achilles anymore? I heard from other bands that ROA couldn't afford paying the studio bills for their records (e.g. Omnium Gatherum) - same problem with you?

Well, "departure" wasn't recorded in a "real" studio and thus there were no bills to pay. The only thing that they said that they would pay was the mastering. Anyways, why Forest of Shadows left Rage of Achilles for Firedoom was because they said that they wouldn't be able to release the album due to financial problems. I was never disappointed at Rage of Achilles and their work and if it wouldn't have been for the financial issue I would probably still be signed to them. They actually helped me by getting in contact with Firebox/Firedoom and thus saved the band from disaster.
What are your plans for the near future? A new album - do you already have some new songs - or adding some members to the band?

There are some unofficial plans for the future that I don't want to get into yet since it will only create alot of expecations. I don't like, or am able for that matter, to work under too much pressure so I will unfold these new plans once I have the new material ready to be recorded. What I can say is that I have more than enough material already for the next release.
Adding new members isn't anything I am planning for. But if someone shows up that has the right attitude and talent then I guess I won't turn that person down.

item.author: Toni
item.p: Greetings from Finland! How's life in the Forest of Shadows camp?
It's quite alright, thanks.
You've finally managed to put out your debut CD, how does it feel?

It feels very nice. Especially due to the circumstances. I mean it was developed over a rather long period of time filled with various kinds of problems that made it all rather painfull. But there's an end to everything and this time it was a happy one.
You did everything on Departure yourself. What was the process like and What are your methods in making an album?

Most of the basic parts of the compositions were done together with former member Micce so I can't take credit for doing it all by myself. Apart from that I did everything by myself and I must say that it wasn't always that funny. I mean when I create music I want it to sound a certain way and if my far from perfected skills restricts me from reaching this goal I get rather frustrated. I guess that's one of the main reason why it all took so long. My ambitions were far higher than my skills and it all ended in alot of recordings until I was satisfied.

My methods for creating an album is of the incremental type. I record alot of simple melodies and harmonies and merge the similar parts to basic song structures. Then I build layer after layer upon each foundation to make it all more complete and unified. Finally I try remove as much as possible apart from the very essential notes so it doesn't get too messy. The actuall recordings are done while the songs are being composed over and over again.
Could you ever think of using outside assistance with FOS albums?

Sure I could if this assistance adds something to the music that can't be added in some other way. I really try to do as much possible by myself since it gives me more control but I'm very aware of the fact that there are limits to my instrumental skills and I don't like to be restricted by such limits.
What kind of a process is songwriting for you?
A rather long and frustrating one since I never get satisfied.
How would you describe the music of FOS?
As dark, depressive, melodic and progressive. By progressive I don't mean pretentious and overdone but more the kind of music that breaks boundraries.
How do people usually react to the music of FOS, and why?

Most people seem to react pretty positive. Even some of thoose who normally don't like the style. I would almost call it easy-listening metal at times. Most people find it rather dark and melancholic though. A bit more than they can handle.
Your music is very dark, sad and cold, in a good way. Is darkness a natural thing for you?

Not natural as in me being a depressed and suicidal person. I'm just attracted the darker kinds of art. Ofcourse I have had my share of grief which function as inspiration but apart from that I'm not that dark minded. I have a rather dark and morbid sense of humour though and I'm very aware of the fact that there is more in life than peace, love and understanding.
Where does this darkness come from?

From within. I never found it hard to get inspiration for making dark music. It's much harder to try and create happy music. More or less impossible I would say since I can't take it serious if it's happy.
What is the very essence of your music and what's your biggest inspiration?
The very essence of my music is darkness in all kinds of forms and my biggest inspiration for this is life itself. Either experience by myself or told by others.
Departure seems to tell a very sad story. Is there a consept behind the album?

The songs weren't composed with a concept i mind but when I rearranged them I tried hard to unify them. I really like albums that are unified no matter if there's a concept behind it all or not. It's a much bigger experience for me to listen to that kind of albums than listening to albums were you have to skip tracks before you reach that special song that is the only reason why you bought the album.
Is the any philosophy behind the lyrics?

Well, there is very much more behind the lyrics than the obvious interpretations. I don't want give away any secrets and thus I leave it to the listener to figure out what the lyrics are really about.
What are the thing you like to write about?

Emotionall stuff. Things that have a meaning to me and that deals with situations in life that were quite hard to get through. It's all very dressed up in fancy and pretentious pseudo-poetry to match the style of the music but the very core of it is rather simple and personal.
Who do you think are the biggest influences in the music of FOS, and why?

That varies with time. For "departure" I would say Opeth, Katatonia, Rapture, Cult of Luna, Massive Attack, Portishead, Anekdoten and Anathema. Why? Well, since they all resembled the kind of music that I wanted to create for this release.
FOS has existed from the year 1997, but it's still fairly unknown here in Finland. Could you tell us something about the history of FOS?

It's not very mysterious that Forest of Shadows isn't that known since there has only been two official releases and both on rather small labels. What has happened since 1997 then. Well, quite alot. It all started in Halmstad as a solo-project of mine with no ambitions whatsoever. After the release of the first demo "The Silent Cry" in 1998 Micce Andersson arrived and made the band to alot more than it used to be. We released two promos "promo '99" and "promo 2000" before we became a stable three-piece with Martin Claesson on guitar. The three of us had the ambition of turning Forest of Shadows into a performing band and we did two gigs with various session members. It didn't go very well though and when Martin left since he didn't share the same vision as me we turned the band into a studio project once again. With alot of success behind us from mp3.com were we had reigned the charts with the songs from the previous two promos we started recording what would be our first offical release, the mCD "where dreams turn to dust", with great motivation. The material ended first up at a small American label called Molded Skull Records but didn't stay there for very long since the label soon ceased to exist due to financial problems. Instead we were picked up by English Rage of Achilles who released it almost instantly. The contract with Rage of Achilles also included a full-length and we started working on this one right after the release of the mCD. Very soon we bumped into both lack of motivation and disagreement of the direction of the music. This resulted in that me and Micce parted and I continued working on what would be "departure" all by myself. When the album after years of hard work was finally finished Rage of Achilles decided to quit due to financial problems. Lucky enough they helped me by getting in contact with a new label, Firebox, who were interested in releasing the album and that's pretty much were we are now.
FOS is now a one-man-band, but it used to have more members. Do you think that FOS works better as a one- man-band, or are you planning on recruiting new members in the future?

There are no plans of getting any new members but if someone shows up that I just can't turn down then I guess I would welcome that person. As it is right now I would say that Forest of Shadows works much better now than it used to do. Mainly because I can fulfill my musical visions without anyone else interfering.
The most important of your old members was Micce Anderson, who left in 2002. What was his place in the band, and why did he leave?

He was the main instrumentalist and a pretty good one as well. It was a pretty hard job to substitute his departure and to some extent I can't say I really managed that well. He's also a schooled musican and he has alot of knowledge in how to make the best out of songs. This means that he was pretty much involved in the arrangements and more or less in charge of the recordings and mixings. Why he left was because he didn't share the same vision as I did. There was no way that we could pull of an album together that we both would have felt was perfectly done. At least we felt that way, or at least I felt that way. Another reason why we split up was that I moved from Gothenburg to Stockholm which made it more complicated to run the band together.
FOS was one of the bands that were on Rage of Achilles' roster when it collapsed. What were your feelings when this happened?

I was ofcourse very sad 'cause for a little while before Firebox showed up I thought that it also meant the end for "departure" and maybe even Forest of Shadows in the long run. It was also very sad since Rage of Achilles was a very interesting label on the rise that delivered music that felt fresh and far from normative. Unfortunatelly this is the reality for most underground labels no matter how good music they deliver.
How do you think that FOS' music will evolve in the future?

Most likely away from the normative metal sound but still with the same dark and melodic core. It's very hard to predict what will really happen though. I just feel that it's hard to do anything new and fresh and still cling to the same old structures and just end up as another perfected clone.
What other plans do you have for the future of FOS?
Nothing more than to keep on producing what I belive is good music.
Do you think that could your music be presented in a live situation, and what would be the ideal situation for it?

It could probably be pretty nice to see the songs performed live and I don't see it as a mission impossible. It would demand quite alot of members though and a certain atmosphere. The ideal situation would be a setup of seven or eight musicans who all know the songs by heart and can perform them with alot of emotion. Such a setup and a dramatic and mysterous place for the actual performance would be quite nice indeed.
Ok, I think that this is enough of questions. Any final words? Maybe a question I didn't ask, but you'd like to answer?
I think you covered quite alot in this interview so I don't have much else to say. Thanx for a nice interview.

item.author: Ruben
item.p: Da jeg hÃ¸rte Forest of Shadows sin debutfullengder for litt siden. Makan til perle av et album er det sjelden man kommer over og man mÃ¥tte selvfÃ¸lgelig finn ut mer om denne mannen og dette bandet! Jeg fikk beskjed om at det Ã¥ fÃ¥ tak i denne mannen pÃ¥ telefon ikke var direkte lett, sÃ¥ det du leser her er gjort via e-mail. Oversettelse fÃ¸ler jeg er unÃ¸dvendig siden det er pÃ¥ svensk og jeg fÃ¸ler at dere fÃ¥r hans svar framfor min oversetting nÃ¥r det gjÃ¸res slik. Uansett, kos dere med svarene fra Niclas Forhagen.
Gratulerer sÃ¥ mye med Â»DepartureÂ», der har du virkelig laget noe fantastisk!
Tack sÃ¥ mycket
Jeg regner med at responsen kun har vÃ¦rt positiv pÃ¥ denne plata? Hvor viktig fÃ¸ler du at responsen fra media er?

Den Ã¤r vÃ¤ldigt viktig. BÃ¥de fÃ¶r mig sjÃ¤lv och fÃ¶r bandets framtida utveckling och dess popularitet. Ãverlag tycker jag att responsen varit positiv men det finns Ã¤ven en del negativa inslag. Negativ respons kan vara bra fÃ¶r den kan peka pÃ¥ saker som verkligen behÃ¶ver fÃ¶rbÃ¤ttras som man kanske inte tÃ¤nkt pÃ¥ sjÃ¤lv. Icke konstruktiv respons Ã¤r mindre kul dock.
Kan du fortelle leserne om Forest of Shadows, hvordan det hele startet etc.?

Bandet grundades i Halmstad 1997 som ett solo-project fÃ¶r att fÃ¥ utlopp fÃ¶r mina musikaliska visioner. UngefÃ¤r ett Ã¥r senare och efter det att den fÃ¶rsta demon slÃ¤ppts gick Micce Andersson med i bandet. Tillsammans lyfte vi bandet frÃ¥n att vara ett obskyrt projekt till att bli ett mer kvalitativt och seriÃ¶st band. Vi spelade in tvÃ¥ stycken promoskivor som cirkulerade pÃ¥ nÃ¤tet och som blev vÃ¤ldigt populÃ¤ra och dÃ¥ frÃ¤mst pÃ¥ mp3.com. SÃ¥ pass populÃ¤ra att de mer eller mindre sÃ¥g till att vi fick skivkontrakt fÃ¶r mCD:n "where dreams turn to dust". FÃ¶rst blev vi kontrakterade av bolaget Molded Skull Records i USA men de hamnade i ekonomiska svÃ¥righeter och fick dra sig ur. Som ersÃ¤ttare dÃ¶k engelska Rage of Achilles upp och rÃ¤ddade mCD:n. VÃ¥rt kontrakt med Rage of Achilles innefattade Ã¤ven en fullÃ¤ngdare som vi bÃ¶rjade jobba pÃ¥ direkt efter mCD:n. Till en bÃ¶rjan gick arbetet med denna skiva vÃ¤ldigt bra men allt eftersom sÃ¥ bÃ¶rjade mina och Micces Ã¥sikter om skivans stil och inriktning glida ifrÃ¥n varandra och detta i takt med att motivationen avtog. Det hela slutade med att Micce lÃ¤mnade bandet och att jag ensam spelade in skivan sÃ¥ som jag ville ha den. Detta visade sig bli en ganska segdragen och plÃ¥gosam process som slutade med att Rage of Achilles hamnade i ekonomiska problem precis dÃ¥ skivan var klar fÃ¶r att slÃ¤ppas. Finska Firebox tog snabbt Ã¶ver ansvaret att slÃ¤ppa skivan och till slut, tre Ã¥r senare kom "departure" ut.
Hvordan har sÃ¥ kreeringen av Departure foregÃ¥tt? Har du gjort om pÃ¥ mye etter at Micce forsvant?

Det mest grundlÃ¤ggande i lÃ¥tarna Ã¤r detsamma som nÃ¤r Micce var med i bandet medan resten har blivit omgjort. Skivan gick totalt genom tre processer. Den fÃ¶rsta Ã¤gde rum ungefÃ¶r samtidigt som mCD:n slÃ¤pptes dÃ¥ vi tillsammans skrev mer eller mindre allt material. Ett halvÃ¥r senare nÃ¤r vi skulle spela in det hela gav vi lÃ¥tarna en rejÃ¤l ansiktslyftning och bytte ut en av lÃ¥tarna mot instrumentala "bleak dormition". NÃ¤r Micce lÃ¤mnade bandet blev det dags fÃ¶r den sista fÃ¶rÃ¤ndringen vilken ocksÃ¥ blev den stÃ¶rsta. Att prova lÃ¥tarna i en massa olika arrangemang var vÃ¤ldigt nyttigt Ã¤ven om det till viss del tog dÃ¶d pÃ¥ de grundlÃ¤ggande ideÃ©rna.
Hvordan trives du med Ã¥ holde pÃ¥ med Forest of Shadows for deg selv?

Jag trivs vÃ¤ldigt bra i denna sits. Forest of Shadows har ju alltid varit mer eller mindre mitt band och det har bara varit vÃ¤ldigt jobbigt nÃ¤r andra har velat styra och stÃ¤lla. Det fanns en tid dÃ¥ jag och Micce fungerade vÃ¤ldigt bra tillsammans men nÃ¤r den tiden tog slut sÃ¥ ville jag inget annat Ã¤n att Forest of Shadows skulle bli mitt solo-projekt igen.
Du har valgt Ã¥ spille melodiÃ¸s doom, hvorfor valgte du akkurat denne formen for musikk? Hvilke inspirasjonskilder har du?

Jag har egentligen inte valt att spela "melodiÃ¶s doom". Det rÃ¥kar bara vara en av mÃ¥nga musikstilar som jag tycker om och dÃ¤rfÃ¶r har den fÃ¤rgat min musik. NÃ¤r jag gÃ¶r musik tÃ¤nker jag inte i banor om att nu skall jag gÃ¶ra doom-metal utan jag gÃ¶r bara vad som kÃ¤nns bra. Ibland blir det doom, ibland mer rockigt och ibland elektroniskt och avskalat. De band som inspirerar mig fÃ¶r tillfÃ¤llet Ã¤r Cult of Luna, Isis, Shape of Despair, Anekdoten, Katatonia och Antimatter. Tidigare har Opeth, My Dying Bride och Anathema varit stora inspirationskÃ¤llor och till viss del lever detta sÃ¤kert kvar Ã¤ven om jag inte tÃ¤nker pÃ¥ det.

NÃ¥r jeg leste tekstene dine fikk jeg en fÃ¸lelse av at du har lest Aaron Stainthorpe fra My Dying Bride sine tekster, vil du pÃ¥stÃ¥ av han i noen grad har pÃ¥virket din mÃ¥te Ã¥ skrive tekster pÃ¥? Er dette et band som har betydd noe for deg?

My Dying Bride har betytt en vÃ¤ldigt massa fÃ¶r mig men jag har aldrig velat kÃ¶pa deras koncept rakt av. Det finns sÃ¤kert en hel del likheter mellan mina och Aarons texter men jag kan lova att det inte Ã¤r avsiktligt. Jag har faktiskt inte lÃ¤st hans texter sÃ¥ vÃ¤ldigt mycket trots att jag lyssnat pÃ¥ My Dying Bride en hel del under Ã¥ren som gÃ¥tt. Anathema och Opeth har nog tilltalat mig mer vad gÃ¤ller lyrik. Den stÃ¶rsta inspirationen till mina texter kommer dock ifrÃ¥n "riktiga" poeter.
Din MCD kom pÃ¥ Firebox mens Departure kom pÃ¥ Firedoom, hva var det som gjorde at det ble slik?
MCD:n kom pÃ¥ Rage of Achilles och inte pÃ¥ Firebox. VarfÃ¶r jag bytte frÃ¥n Rage of Achilles till Firebox/Firedoom var fÃ¶r att RoA fick problem med ekonomin och inte kunde slÃ¤ppa skivan.

NÃ¥r jeg hadde hÃ¸rt gjennom Departure en del ganger satt jeg igjen med en fÃ¸lelse av at du har latt deg bli inspirert av Antimatter/Massive Attack. Er dette noe du kan bekrefte eller er det bare tilfeldig? Hva er det sÃ¥ i tilfellet som gjÃ¸r at du har blandet inn slik lett Â»industrielleÂ» biter i musikken?

Visst har jag blivit inspirerad av band som Massive Attack, Antimatter, Portishead och liknande. De spelar ju faktiskt vÃ¤ldigt "doomy" musik sÃ¥ det kÃ¤ndes inte helt konstigt att nyttja denna inspiration i Forest of Shadows.
Har Forest of Shadows holdt noen konserter? Vil du eventuelt gjÃ¸re det i framtiden og hvordan vil du da gjÃ¸re det med folk? Har du noen faste live-session musikere?

Vi gjorde tvÃ¥ konserter 1999 och 2000 men de var inte speciellt lyckade. Under 2001 fÃ¶rsÃ¶kte vi sÃ¤tt ihop ett band fÃ¶r att kunna gÃ¶ra fler och bÃ¤ttre spelningar men det hela rann ut i sanden. DÃ¤refter sÃ¥ har det inte funnits nÃ¥gra tankar pÃ¥ att spela live igen. Men vem vet vad som hÃ¤nder i framtiden.
Har du noe nytt materiale klart? Hvordan er det eventuelt i forhold til det materialet vi finner pÃ¥ Departure?

Det finns vÃ¤ldigt mycket material som bara ligger och vÃ¤ntar. Vad det blir av det Ã¥terstÃ¥r att se. Jag har en del planer men inga som Ã¤r officiella. Det nya materialet kommer nog att fortsÃ¤tta i samma riktning som "departure".

Hva synes du om dagens doom scene? Det er jo sinnsykt mange nye og spennende band synes jeg og det er blitt utrolig hÃ¸y kvalitet over mange av de siste utgivelsene, da spesielt med tanke pÃ¥ Firebox/Firedoom som spesielt har vist seg Ã¥ vÃ¦re et selskap Ã¥ holde Ã¸ynene Ã¥pne for! Hva er dine kommentarer til dette?

Jag Ã¤r inte sÃ¥ insatt i doom scenen. Lyssnar mest pÃ¥ rÃ¥, primitiv och skitig musik. Dvs allt ifrÃ¥n Darkthrone till Isis och Cult of Luna. MÃ¥nga doom band tenderar att bli allt fÃ¶r pretantiÃ¶sa pÃ¥ ett dÃ¥ligt sÃ¤tt vilket fÃ¶rstÃ¶r hela grejen. Ett doom band som Ã¤r grymt bra Ã¤r dock Shape of Despair. De har verkligen hittat ett grymt sound som Ã¤r bÃ¥de unikt och tungt.
Har du noe avsluttende ord til vÃ¥re lesere?
Jag skulle vilja ge ett stort tack till alla som stÃ¶ttat Forest of Shadows pÃ¥ ett eller annat vis och nu eller i framtiden.
Jeg vil takke deg for at du har tatt deg tid til Ã¥ svare pÃ¥ mine spÃ¸rsmÃ¥l og ikke minst for Ã¥ ha laget Ã¥rets beste Doom plate i form av Departure og lykke til videre med Forest of Shadows!!!

item.author: Jan Fischer
item.p: What happened between the release of the mini-cd "When Dreams Turn To Dust" and your latest release "Departure"? What exactly happened to Rage Of Achilles?

We started working on the material for "departure" even before the release of the mCD and it was initially scheduled for a release in early 2002. Even though there was very good progress in the beginning things soon came to a stall. We were both lacking motivation and there was some disagreement about what it all should sound like. Eventually, during the summer of 2002, we decided to get it all over with so we started recording. It didn't go very well and when I some month later moved from gothenburg to stockholm it all felt like it would never be finished. That was when I decided that I'd better try and finish it all by myself and thus Micce had to go. What happened then was a long process or rearranging the songs and learning how to play them, followed by a pretty long recording session that lasted for like a year until I was satisfied. When everything was recorded and ready to be released the worst I could imagine happened and that was that Rage of Achilles had to pull out because of financial problems. They helped me finding another label (Firebox) pretty quick though so the disaster didn't last very long.
Your band has been in existence for some years now. Looking back, what would you have done differently?

That's a tricky one. I mean I'm pretty satisfied with what has been accomplished so far and I don't know if there is anything that could have been done that would have improved the situation. Maybee it would have been for the best if the cooperation with Micce had ended some months earlier than it did 'cause in the end we didn't accomplish much of value together.
Seems like you are the only (constant) member of Forest Of Shadows. Why is that?

Because I'm the sole founding member I guess. Forest of Shadows has always been my band to some extent and I guess I'm not the easiest person to cooperate with. At least not when it comes to personal things such as music. I guess that pretty much explains why there has been so many members coming and going.
Who is the person on the inner cd cover? Any story connected to that? Looks like some randomly taken foto of some dictator...
That's me, the bands dictator. Pretty funny actually since I have been acting like one while there has been other members in the band. I never thought of that when I inserted the photo though.
On a typical Saturday evening, you ...

...don't do much. Spend time with my girlfriend and dog. Play games, watch tv or some movie. Every now and then I go out for some beer with close friends or even attend to some concert but otherwise it's pretty laid back.

The music of Forest Of Shadows is dark and melancholic. There are a couple of other bands that play that or some similar style; Katatonia or Rapture come to mind. How do you perceive those bands and what distinguishes your music from theirs?

I wouldn't say that they are that similar. There are ofcourse passages in my music that resembles these bands but generally speaking it's quite different. I do like them both though and they are some of the main influences when I write music. What distinguishes my music from theirs is, in my humble opinion, the song structures and the diversity. I would dare say that Forest of Shadows is more progressive while Rapture and Katatonia stick to their special sound. Most likely that will be the case for Forest of Shadows as well one day.
What are the plans for the band? Will you stay with Firedoom Music? Will there ever be a tour outside of Sweden?

The contract with Firedoom was only for the full-length so I don't really know what will happen there. I don't see any reason not to stick with Firedoom if they give me a new contract though. Apart from that I don't have any official plans right now. I trying to relax after the dramtic time with "departure" and gain some energy for the next release whatever that will be.
There will not be any tour outside sweden or in sweden for that matter as it looks right now. I can't say if there will be one ever. The future will tell.
Something that people don't know about Stockholm but should know in your opinion?
That we don't have icebears walking in the streets.
When you hear your own music, what do you feel?

Alot of things. It often brings back old memories of the time when the music was created. Most of the time I get a certain degree of satisfaction from what I have accomplished. There are actually few things apart from some details that I am feel ashamed of. I always find it strange to hear other musicans who can't bear to listen to their own music.

item.author: Fernando
item.p: How does it feel to release an album which the music and the lyrics were developed for the last seven years?

It was actually just one of the songs that had been around for seven years. The other songs were born around the time when we released the mCD. Anyway, it feels really good to be able to add these songs to the bands history and focus on new material.
Do you find an identity on the whole record, or does it look to you more like a compilation of different songs, feelings and times?

In some way you may say that there is a shared identity. I mean the lyrics are more or less about the same topic and the songs are composed and arranged as a unity. Still, the songs where written and arranged over a long period of time during which I have had lots of different sources of inspiration. It's a unity on the surface and more of a compilation on the inside.
Where did you record all these tracks? Do you use your own studio?

It was all recorded in my apartment in Stockholm. I don't know if I can say that I have a real studio there but I have enough equipment to be able to pull off a recording session. It's far from fancy and it sure restricts the quality of the album in terms of production but well, it works.
Is owning a studio a good thing when you do doom metal music? I mean, is it necessary a kind of own private environment to perform and write this music at it's best?

There are pros and cons of having a "studio" at home. The good thing about it is like you say the possibillity to record whenever you feel like it and when you feel inspired. The disadvantages are that you never leave your "work" which means that it's very easy to start overdoing stuff. It's also a problem that you can't record whenever you want to because of your neighbours.
You're alone in this project now. Advantages and disadvantages of working that way, please...

The big advantage is that I can follow my visions and fulfill them without any interferance. It's also very nice to be able to decide when I want to work with Forest of Shadows or when I need to put it all on ice in order to be able to work on other things. The disadvantage is that it's very time consuming to do everything by yourself. It's also very hard to perform well on all instruments.
Again, does the fact that you work - compose and perform - alone enable you to achieve the right mood for doing this specific kind of music?

Even when there were other members in the band I prefered to write everything by myself in solitude. It sure makes it easier to bring out that special mood. Performing alone is also pretty good since it's more relaxing. A bad thing about performing alone is that it's quite messy to handle the recordings and the instruments at the same time. It's also nice if someone else can judge whether or not the recordings are good.
Are you a naturally melancholic person, or does this project help you getting rid of that part of your personality and make you a somehow "happier" and "more sociable" person?

Deep inside I don't think I'm more melancholic than anyone else. The big difference between me and the "happy" people is that I accept the fact that there is a melancholic side of me that I can't get rid of and thus have to live with. I don't know if I get "happier" by letting out my feelings or "more sociable" for that matter. Though, writing dark and depressive music is a way of accepting and revealing these parts of me and I guess that's a good thing for me as a person.
You mix not-so-usual elements in your music, as clean vocals and slight electronic elements on rhythms. How did you get to this style?

I wouldn't say that clean vocals is that very avantgarde in the realms of extreme metal these days and actually not electronic elements either. Maybee I use them a bit more than others do which gives my music a certain character. Why I choose to add these elements is 'cause I thought they fit the compositions. When I compose music I don't really care if it's going to end up as old-school doom or electronica. I Just do what feels right. On the next release I might add even stranger elements or maybe I'll just go old-school. Who knows?

How I got into clean vocals and electronica? Well, I actually listened to synth and pop-music before I got into metal so I guess I have it natural. I'm pretty far from a die-hard metal person. I like dark music that's all. I don't care if it's metal, rock, goth, pop, synth, electronica, trip-hop, indie or whatever.
What's your opinion about the usual puritists that will say this is not doom metal and that you should call your music something else?

I'm not the one that label my music "doom metal" so I'm not the one they should bark at. I totally agree that Forest of Shadows isn't a "true" doom-metal band. My opinion about these puritists is that they are rather pathetic that care more about a certain label than the actual music. And who are they to say what I am allowed to call my music or not. If these persons could stop being so narrowminded they would probaly discover that there is alot of "doomy" bands out there apart from the puristic ones.
Do your inspiration - for music and lyrics - come naturally, when you want, or do you have to be with a special feeling to allow these things to come to you?

It just comes, regardless if I want to or not, if I'm in some kind of emotional state. Listening to new fresh music adds alot to the inspiration as well. To get inspiration for arranging and producing music is pretty easy and I don't even have to be in a special mood for that. The difficult one is the basic harmonies and melodies as well as the lyrics.
Do you have any kind of technique - or quimical help - to allow the inspiration to come?
For lyrics it might help to read books and other bands lyrics. For music I just need some solitude with my piano or guitar and I sooner or later will come up with something.
You do have other musical projects, right? Which ones are you doing these days, and how do you split your time between them?

Yeah, there are some other project in which I am involved but most of them are on ice right now. First of all there is "Ningizzia" which invloves me and Stephane Peudupin. I have spent alot of time with this band during the years. For periods I worked 50-50 with Forest of Shadows and Ningizzia. Ningizzia is also quite similar to Forest of Shadows but a bit more diverse. Then there is Shubend (/Genesis of Pain) which used to be a rehearsing band with five members when I lived in Gothenburg. After I moved I haven't spent much time with this band apart from writing some lyrics every now and then. Shubend isn't similar to Forest of Shadows at all apart from the fact that I'm doing the vocals. It's more of a technical thrash/death band.
You drawn a logo to Sweet Flame, from Portugal. Do you know anything further from the Portuguese scene?

Not very much. I used to listen to Moonspell quite alot but I guess that's all. I'm not that well oriented in the metal scene these days no matter which country it concerns apart from the scandinavian countries.

item.author: Andi
item.p: Hei Niclas, how are you?
I'm pretty fine thanx.
Can you give us a short overview about yourself as a person and about your musical work with Forest Of Shadows?

Well, I'm 29 years old and living in Stockholm, Sweden together with my girlfriend and dog. I have a masters degree in computer science and work as a programmer/sound director at a game developer. Forest of Shadows has been a project of mine to satisfy the aestetic side of me and it has moved through alot of phases during the year ranging from solo-project to five-piece band. The style and direction has always been the same though and will probably always be.
It has happened again: The press lavishes you on doxologies. Did you expect this positive response? How does that feel?

It's ofcourse very nice to know that people appreciate my work. A part of me didn't expect this response since the music contains alot of elements that lies far from the genres normative. But I'm glad that my fans aren't as narrowminded as I had feared. I guess that's what makes me most happy.
How did you get in contact with Firedoom?

When Rage of Achilles closed down, Duncan, their manager, helped me out to get a new contract with another label. He knew Rami at Firebox since they had been distributing Rage of Achilles albums in Finland and he introduced Forest of Shadows to him. Rami got interested and we soon inked a deal. Some month later Rami started Firedoom as a subdivision to Firebox and let Forest of Shadows be one of the first bands to release anything on this label. I felt very honoured.
I read that the work on "Departure" took three years. Why?

Well, me and former member Micce spent about a year to try and produce the album and it didn't go very well for various reasons where the main one was that we couldn't agree about what the album should sound like. It all ended up with Micce leaving the band and I had to finish the production by myself. Since I wasn't satisfied with the arrangements I redid them all which took quite some time. Then I had to learn to play everything which was quite hard since Micce had been the main instrumentalist in the band. I also had to spend alot of time messing around with the sound to get everything sounding the way I wanted it to sound. So it took quite alot of time.
How was it - working together with Mika Jussila?

I didn't really work with Mika. I just sent him the material and he added some final touches based on a wishlist I had made. Unfortuantelly he managed to push the levels a bit too much which resulted in some unwanted distortions. Apart from that I think he did a good job.
What inspires you when you're writing songs?

I don't know. Different emotional states, personal experiences and stuff like that. I don't get that inspired by other peoples work, well not on a low level anyway. It's really hard to know what actually causes your musical visions. They just come.
Do you have a stable line up when you're producing an album?
No. There has always been people coming and going during the producitons. The only one where that didn't happen was the '99 promo aka "The Silent Cry".
The live realisation: How do you plan to do that? Up to now you're a one man project.
I don't. It would have been fun to perform the songs live but as it is right now I don't see that happening ever.
When you wrote "Departure", what have been your influences. (I can't help but sometimes it remembers me of Massive Attack's "Mezzanine".)

I've been inspired by Massive Attack, Portishead and Earthling and stuff like that. They're not my main sources of inspirations but they sure added a certain touch to the sound. The more important bands that influenced the produciton was: Cult of Luna, Isis, Katatonia, Anekdoten and Shape of Despair.
The response to "Where Dreams Turn To Dust" has been very positiv some years ago. Regarding to that, was it difficult to try to top that?

Departure is not an attempt to top that album. It's a different album with different values and should be regarded as such. Of course I tried to write better lyrics and better music as well as giving it all a better performance. If I succeeded or not is very personal. I think it's better album but I guess alot of people would sy the opposite. For some reason people have a way of clinging onto the old material. There are even people who think that the first demo marks the peak of Forest of Shadows career. And why not? I'm the kind of person myself who think that alot of bands did their best stuff on their first albums even though their later material is far better in many way.
You have another band called "Shubend", haven't you? Tell us what's behind it!

I used to do the vocals in a band called Shubend. I'm saying "used to" since the band has more or less ceased to exist. We recorded our swansong album some months ago and are still trying to get it released in some way. Previous to that we had released one full-length "Synergism" and two demos "Singularis" and "Hook, Line and Sinker". Be warned though. Shubend is not very similar to Forest of Shadows at all. It's more of a technical thrash/death band. The only thing that unites the bands are the fact that I do the vocals in them both.
Music seems to be very important to you - what else is important to you, apart from music?
My close ones. Without any competition. Apart from that I'm I would say movies and visual art.
What can we expect next from you? Any plans?
There are some plans but I don't want to make them official yet.

item.author: Alex Straka
item.p: Hi Niclas! First of all tell me about your inner life. "Departure" is blasting by an unbelievable sadness. Are you ok?

Concerning my inner life I would like to keep that private. I hope you respect that. Anyways, I'm not as down and depressed as you might get the illusion of from the album. You know, in order to make melancholic music you don't have to be melancholic. You just need to have a dark and twisted mind. It helps though if you know what it's like to be down and belive me I do know what that's like.
FOREST OF SHADOWS has a history full of changes of the line-up. Now you act alone. Tell me about please.

Well, I guess I'm rather hard to work with at least if you want to change the direction of my music. I guess it all comes down to that I started the band as my solo-project and thus I will always consider it my band. Not very democratic I know. Being the only member, as it is right now, has its pros and cons. On the negative side lies the fact that I need to do everything by myself which is very time consuming and restricting. Still, this is something I can live with at the cost of having full control not only over the music and visual artworks but also when and how I am to work with FOREST OF SHADOWS.
How you create your songs? This almighty melancholy needs a special daily form or?
Writing lyrics and the basic harmonies and melodies do need a certain daily form, a less happy one. The rest can be done whenever. I know pretty well how I want it to sound.
The songs of "Departure" are not the newest ones. WhatÂ´s the reason, you can record the album not earlier?

Correction: The songs on "departure" are the newest ones. Well, apart from "november dream" which is an old demo song from 1997. Still, this remake differs quite alot from the original so it might as well be considered a new song. Why I, or actually we (Micce was also in the band we took the decision), choose to include "november dream" on the album is because it's a good song that deserves a better destiny than the shitty one it got by ending up on the first demo, a demo that was far from well produced.
What do you await now from the release of "Departure", in my opinion a masterpiece of doomy dark metal?

Masterpiece of doomy dark metal. Now that's a nice compliment. What I await from the new album? I don't know actually. Guess I'm more than happy if people just like it. I'm not really expecting a grammy or anything :)

There are enough doom metal bands around the world. But on "Departure" rules the highest score of intensity IÂ´ve ever heared. Forced by the continued setlist, you play often with the levels, with loudness and silence. Added the variance of your vocals, FOREST OF SHADOWS sounds very similary. The only band you compare I think is WHILE HEAVEN WEPT from the states. What do you think about this?
I don't know much about WHILE HEAVEN WEPT but I guess I heard them once and since I didn't remember them all that well I assume they didn't sound that similar to FOREST OF SHADOWS.
Niclas, you are songwriter, arrangeur, producer, mixer, engineer and creative master in personal union. Too much time ;-)? WhatÂ´s the reason you make these jobs alone?

If there's something I don't have then it's too much time. My health is actually pretty worn down by being involoved in three bands while working and trying maintain a social life. Still, I really value being involved in as much as possible when it comes to FOREST OF SHADOWS no matter if it's writing music, performing it or doing graphical works for booklets or websites. I guess the main reason is that every time that I have involved other people it has ended up with them wanting to change things and forming the band into something I can't relate to. I might be an egocentric dictator but FOREST OF SHADOWS means too much for me and I don't see any point in keeping the band alive if I can't realte to it 100%. After all it's band I formed to express my own musical visions. If I found people to work with that would be willing to just obey my orders then I might consider working with them but otherwise I rather do it all by myself no matter if it all takes alot more time and lacks of technical quality.
Please tell me about the recordings of the album. At final "Departure" was mastered by Mr. Jussila at Finnvox, right?

Well, it was actually recorded two times. One with Micce as a member of the band and one after he had left. We started out writing new material even before the mCD was released and had the basic structures of all songs to be included in the original tracklist finished some month later. Then we entered a pretty uncreative and less motivating phase of finalizing the songs. The main reason to this was that we had big problems with merging our visions of what the final material should be like. We didn't work on the material on a daily basis, not even a weekly. After a while we started to grow tired of it all and decided to do something about it. So we started doing the final recordings. The first step was to record the drums and we did this in a small studio called NoiseLab that some friends of mine used to have. The three days we spent in the studio were filled with all kinds of problems and we almost gave up on the second day. Finally we managed to pull it all together though but the result wasn't very satisfying. After some weeks of trying to make it sound good we finally abandoned the drum recordings and replaced them with programmed ones. This failure had quite an impact on the rest of the recordings and soon it was progressing at snailpace again. When I decided to move from Gothenburg to Stockholm some month later I started to realize that the only way this album would be finished would be if either me or Micce did it seperatelly since the distance geographically and in musical visions was to big. So unfortuatelly Micce had to go. Loosing Micce wasn't easy though since he was the main musican in the band. I had to spend alot of time learning how to play the stuff before I could start recording it. It took a year and a half until it was all recorded and mixed and ready to be sent to mr Jussila. Mr Jussila did a pretty good job I must say even though the levels were pushed a bit too far at some places which produced unwanted distortions.

The complete conception (music, photos, artwork etc.) of FOREST OF SHADOWS is focussed on your dark, hopeless and sad lyrics. How much time you invest in your project. It sounds like your whole soul ;-).

When I started Forest of Shadows I wanted to start a band that was dark and melancholic in a unified way. I was kind of irritated at bands that couldn't match their dark music with equally dark lyrics and artworks. I'm not saying that I'm the ultimate artist but at least my vision were and is to create art that is as dark and melancholic as possible in all possible ways. I guess alot of bands are scared of being labeled pretentious or pathetic and thus doesn't go all the way with their dark and melancholic art. Or maybee they just have bad taste :) To answer that last question I do spend my entire soul, at least the dark side of it, in my music.
Have you any other material to record in the near future?
Yes, I have lots. I'm in a rather busy phase of my life right now though so I haven't been able to work much with this new material.
Is FOREST OF SHADOWS a studio project or do you come over with a band at short time?
Right now it's as much studio project as it possibly can get. So don't expect any live performances.
Niclas, why our readers must know FOREST OF SHADOWS?

If you're into dark and melancholic music you might find something in Forest of Shadows but if not then you might be better of not knowing about Forest of Shadows at all. I really don't think that my music suits the average metalhead.
At last a personal question. What do you think is the meaning of life and what is the aim in your life?

I don't think there is a certain meaning of life generally speaking apart from the one you create by yourself. Some people that can handle this fact try to find a meaning in supernatural beliefs of all kinds but I rather not. I don't know if I have a ceratin aim in life apart from trying to live in harmony with myself and my close ones.
Niclas, thanx for the intie. I wish you all the best with "Departure", an album full of soul and heartblood. Any last words for our readers?
Thanx for interview Alex and thanx to all you readers for reading the interview.

item.author: Olivier (Zoltar) Badin
item.p: Is there any specific reference behind the name of the band?
No, I wouldn't say there is. It was just a name that I came up with. Later on I tried to give a meaning but initially there was none.
Was at first FOREST OF SHADOWS meant to be a band or was it right from the start bound to be just a personal project?
When I started Forest of Shadows I did it just for the fun of it. Ofcourse I wanted it to be something more than just a personal project but I didn't expect that to happen.
How do you differ FOS from your other musical projects?

Well, FOS is my first and most personal band and thus I put more of myself into it than the other ones. There were periods though when I felt that I couldn't express all my musical visions because of the other members in the band. During these periods NINGIZZIA, another band of mine, became the band in which I could express myself freely. At an early stage there wasn't much difference between the musical visions of these two bands so if I had an idea and it wasn't welcome in one of the bands then I just tried it out in the other one. The third project that I assume you have in mind is the thrash/death band SHUBEND. This band differs quite alot from the other two and it was more of a band that I was in than a band that I lived for. After all I only did the lyrics, well not only since I put quite alot of energy and thought behind them.
Now with a step back, would you say that Â´Where Dreams Trun To DustÂª was then a good representation of FOS or was an unacheived one?

It was definatelly a good representation of FOS at that time even though it could have been done much better. I mean you have to consider that it was done under very poor circumstances. Technically there's also quite alot to wish for but well, that was then and now is now. I'm still very proud of that release and the popularity it achived.
Did you settled right from the start on writing very long and epic tracks?

I have always been fond of long and epic tracks and I like things to last for more than just a few minutes. When I write a song I don't focus on making it long and epic. That's just something that happens.
Were you bothered by the tag of being just another early KATATONIA clone?!
I never heard that we were a KATATONIA clone and I really don't think that we sound much like them. No matter what I could be offended by much worse things so I really don't care.
Why was it so difficult to write the follow-up to this mini CD?

The actual composition of the material wasn't that hard. Most of the melodies and structures were there just a few month after the release of the mCD. What took time was when we started to get into the details of it all. Our visions didn't really point in the same direction and the material meant too much for both of us. Making sacrifices wasn't really an option. After some time of struggling we started to grow tired of it all and more or less just wanted it to be over and done with. This didn't speed up things though. It was first when I decided that the only solution was that I made it all by myself that things started moving.
Â´DepartureÂª seems to be strangely estranged between funeral doom and dark trip-hop. Do you agree?
To some extent yes. I mean it shows some of the outer boundaries of the music. It's not very fair to describe the music with just these two styles. There's so much more involved.
Did you get inspired by MASSIVE ATTACK's Â´Tear DropÂª in order to write Â´Sleeping DeathÂª?

I might have got some inspiration from MASSIVE ATTACK but not from the song "Tear Drop". They have done far doomier songs than that one. Actually, the part that you are refering to wasn't supposed to sound the way it does initially. It wasn't even meant to have any elements of electronica at all. I guess I was very inspired by electronica (and trip-hop if you prefer) when I rearranged that song. I'm really happy about what I did since it gives the song some extra character.
Is Â´DepartureÂª only focusing only how to deal with the loss of the loved ones?

That would be the obvious intepretation but there's more to it. I would say it's more about leaving or be left on an abstract plane. It could be about death, it could be about breaking up, it could be about moving on in life or whatever you want.
Why this album is a mix of newer and older songs, some written in the very early stage of FOS?

I have promised myself and some of my fans that the songs that were recorded on the early demos should get released properly in due time. I started with "November Dream" on this album and will include another one of the demo songs on the next release. It's not only about principles but also about the fact that I really like these old songs and want to give them a better destiny than the horrible one they got on the demo.
Were you influenced by the extreme doom scene?!

A little bit maybee. I can't say that there are that many bands to get inspried from in the extreme doom scene any more. The only ones I can come to think of are Shape of Despair, Evoken and Funeral Orchestra.
Do you see yourself more as a melancolic or as a depressive personne? And how did it affected Â´DepartureÂª?

I don't see myself as melancholic or depressive. There were times when I sure did and I guess that will always affect my music to some extent. Most of the stuff on "Departure" was written during these depressive moments and I guess that made them to what they are. I don't belive that you have to be depressive to write true depressive music though. It's more about knowing what it's like.
Final last words?!
Thanx for the interview and stay doomed

item.author: Christophe
item.p: Hello guys ! How are you by now having a new album released so far?

Hey Innate. The release of the new album is going just fine. So far I haven't read anything negative about it apart from some small details. This is kind of interessting since it's far from a normative metal album or doom-metal album for that matter. I guess the audience is less narrowminded these days which is rather nice.
Staying in the past can you remember for us how it all began? In which bands were ou playing before creating this new act?

It all began in 1997 as a solo-project of mine. I had no experience from any other bands before this date. My only experience was that I had been making quite alot of electronic music on the computer.
Where Dreams Turns To Dust was released by Rage Of Achilles, why did you decided to not continue with this label?

They were supposed to release the full-length but couldn't because of financial problems so there wasn't much of a choice but to turn to another label and in this case the Finnish label Firebox/Firedoom. I was very satisfied with Rage of Achilles and I had never any plans to turn to another label. Still, I'm very lucky that I ended up at Firebox/Firedoom since they are more oriented towards the kind of music that Forest of Shadows play.
In 2002 Micce Anderson left the band, what was exactly his implication in Departure ? Was it a hard situation to continue without him?

Even though all the songs were rearranged quite alot after Micce's departure he still contributed to very much of what you hear on the album. Micce is a very talented person in many ways an he brought alot of key elements to the album. While in the band, Micce was responsible for most of the instruments which made things rather complicated after he left. Even though the music isn't very technical it was technical enough to make the recording sessions a major pain in the ass for me.
This new album was a long work and your biography says that it was rewritten 3 times. Can you explain us why?

Well, me and Micce started working on the material around the time when the mCD was released. It didn't take us very long to have the basic ideas arranged but when we started to get into the details we soon ran out of motivation. We started to work on the material more seldom and the span of time between our sessions increased. When we were getting close to the final recording sessions we both felt (I guess) that we weren't all that satisfied with what we had accomplished in terms of the arrangements. This was also the beginning of the end of our cooperation since we had different ideas of how to arrange it all. Still we managed to rewrite it all before we were supposed to record it. In the middle of the recording session Micce left the band and I had to complete it all by myself. Since I felt that most of the things had to be rerecorded anyway I thought that I might as well rewrite it all the way I wanted it. Can't say that it was an optimal way of putting together an album.
Was it important to rewrite things? Do you believe that the same Â« head Â» can have 3 different ways to write things?

Due to the circumstances I belive that every rearrangement was of importance. I belive that you can rewrite things more than 3 times. There is always room for improvements. The danger of rewriting is that you tend to drift away from the basic idea of the song.
The usual affirmation is that the upcoming cd is always better than the previous one. Don't you think it's a bit annoying to hear that from musicians each time?

Whatever someone says about his/hers work is their bussiniess. I don't find it annoying at all. I mean if you don't belive that you are getting any better then there isn't much point in going on, is there? At least not with the same band.
Actually is the label of Doom music correct for you? According to all the elements on the stuff is there not another way to describe Forest Of Shadows?

Just like most other musicans I really don't like to put a label on my music. And if I had to then I wouldn't say it's doom. There are doomy elements in the music of Forest of Shadows but there is also so much more. Dark metal or just metal would suit much better since they are both more abstract. I actually don't like to be labeled metal either since it connects my music with music that is pretty far from what I create but I guess I have to live with that as long as I use distorted guitars and heavy drums.
I suppose you have others interests than music in life, can you tell us what you love to do when you are not playing or working for the band?
Music isn't really my work. It's actually pretty far from it which means that music is more what I love to do when I'm not working or maintaining my social life.
Metal music is always having new fashions coming and going, for you will it be always like this with new styles that are supposed to come again and again?
There will always be good albums that will insprire and be a part of the current fashion. Some sources of inspiration run dry pretty fast while other can last for ages.
For years black, thrash, death, heavy metal fans seemed divided in their categories, do you have the impression this fact is evolving with time?

Evolving towards what? I do belive that they are drifting apart and that's nothing I care the least about. The only thing some of the bands in the different genres share in common is he fact that they use guitars and drums.
For you what are the 10 most importants cds in metal history? The ones each new metal fan can't understand to metal without knowing it?
To me the 10 most important metal albums are the following (in no particular order).

Opeth - "Morningrise", Anathema - "The Silent Enigma", Katatonia - "Brave Murder Day", Cult of Luna - "The Beyond", Shape of Despair - "Shades of...", Katatonia - "Last Fair Deal Gone Down", My Dying Bride - "Turn Loose the Swans", Opeth - "Blackwater Park", Theatre of Tragedy - "Velvet Darkness They Fear" and Celestial Season - "Solar Lovers".
Innate is based in France, so i would like to have your opinion on our scene there. Do you know so much bands from our country? Labels? Magazines?

I own a couple of albums by Misanthrope and other Holy Recs bands I find them all quite good. Apart from that I know Despond, which is a pretty good doom band. The man behind Despond, Richard, is also running the frensh label Haceldama with a friend of mind, Stephane. Me and Stephane is having a band together called Ningizzia. Ningizzia released a demo thru Impaler of Trendies once upon a time which is the third frensh label I know, or knew. Regarding magazines I can't say that I know any at all. Stephane and Max (of Impaler of Trendies) used to run a zine called Ocean Morphique that I also did some graphics for but that pretty much it.
Do you feel that the politic in general, the world's situation of today is a thing you are caring about? If yes which movements are at the moment the ones you are agreeing with?

I care quite alot both about politics and the situation of the world but I do it as a private person and not a musician. Well, at least that's the case with Forest of Shadows. I have written some lyrics with the bands Shubend and Genesis of Pain that are quite political but in an abstract way. I'm not the kind of person who want to belong to a certain movement or idea and I sure don't want Forest of Shadows to be a political band.
What are the expectations for the band on your personal level?
Not much. I expect only to be able to keep releasing albums as long as I am worthy of doing so.
To end the discussion do you think we had forgot something important to talk about? A last word to the readers?
Check out the new album "departure" and give it a few listenings.
Your wishes for 2004???
That "departure" continues being well received and gets good critics.

item.author: Martin Lazarte Castro
item.p: Can you make a brief story of the band's beginning?

The band was formed back in 1997 with me as the only member. I recorded the demo "The Silent Cry" in 1998 which despite of it's horrible production came to interest multiinstrumentalist Micce Andersson who joined the band shortly there after. We did a promo-cd in early 1999 titled "The Silent Cry", mostly because it featured a remake of the old demo song. One may also say that it was a silent cry to some labels that we wanted to work with :) During 2000 we recorded yet another promo with yet another remake called "Under the Dying Sun". The two remakes were uploaded to mp3.com and gained increasing popularity. In the meantime we teamed up with Martin Claesson on guitar and started rehearsing with ambitions of becoming a live band. Two less successfull gigs later we dropped this ambition and ended the cooperation with Martin some month later. Instead of being a live band we became a 100% studio band and started working on a new demo right away which ended up in the mCD "Where Dreams Turn To Dust". With this new album and the growing popularity on mp3.com we started attracting record companies and in 2001 we signed with Rage of Achilles. The deal included a release of the mCD (previously demo) "Where Dreams Turn To Dust" and a full-length. While the mCD had been recorded pretty quick the full-length evolved very slow even though the material was more or less there as we signed the deal with Rage of Achilles. During this slow progress me and Micce started drifting apart and one day I realized that there was no way we could do this new album together and make it good so despite his importance to the band Micce had to leave. Even though the full-length was already delayed I decided to redo it all from scratch in order to make it all the way I wanted it. And this is what I'm still doing even though I am reaching completion now.
Who is responsible for music and lyrics on the band?
With me as the only member I guess the answer is pretty simple :) When Micce was in the band he was pretty involved in the music but I guess I have had the main responsibillity all the time.
Where do you get inspiration for your lyrics?
Poetry, other doomy lyrics, life, death... from everywhere and nowhere.
What shows have you played that stood out in your mind?
None I guess...
If you were able to obtain immortality, how would you put to use it advantages?

By being (or trying to be) an artist I do become immortal in some non-material sense. Anyway, if I would have been immortal, physically, then I guess I would have tormented people forever and ever with my music :) Honestly, I don't see that many advantages with being immortal if everone I love is mortal...
What do you think about religion, do you think that religion is a dangerous element in mankind's life, why?

I think that everyone has a right to their own belief no matter what it is. Individual beliefs are pretty harmless I would say but big collective religions are quite the opposite. Religion can be the most lethal weapon to mankind but also a saviour. I guess the main problem isn't religion in itself but people more or less forcing others to think and act in a certain way.
Tell me about the band's musical diversity.
Well, what can I say. A one way lane will make me insane :)
What do you know of Peru or Southamerica?
Apart from the geography and that there is alot of problems of various kinds I don't know too much.
Do you have any new release, are you working with a new label, how are you promoting your band?

I am and have been working on a full-length since late 2000. It will most likely be finished sometime during early 2004. I am still working with Rage of Achilles and I have no plans of contacting any other label. I don't promote the band apart from having a website.
Do you have any interest in occultism, will you practice any ritual, why?
I sure am interested and fascinated by occultism but I don't practice anything or belong to any group/sects/movement/circle/whatever.
Any future plan?
To finish the full-length which is more or less killing me...
Any last comment?
Check out the full-length when it's finished. I hope it won't disappoint you.

item.author: AndrÃ© Groeneveld
item.p: Hello Niclas, thanks for doing an interview with us. Could you first introduce Forest Of Shadows to us?

Forest of Shadows is a band devoted to the darker kinds of music. The band was founded in 1997 and has released 5 demos/promos where the final one resulted in a deal with English Rage of Achilles who released this demo as a mCD. The deal also included a full-length which currently is being finialized and will be titled "departure".

Forest Of Shadows started as a solo project and the idea of playing live resulted in searching and finding other musicians. How does it come why there were no other musicians when you started this band? Was it hard to find them or were the ones you know not interested?

When I founded the band I lived in a pretty small town and I really had no hope of finding anyone who would share my musical visions. Especially since I hardly knew anyone there who was into metal music at all.
Did you play in other bands at that time? What about now, do you play in other bands at the moment or is Forest Of Shadows your main focus?

Back then when I started the band I had just ended a hopeless attempt of putting a band together with some friends and that failure was partly the reason why I founded Forest of Shadows. Some months after I founded Forest of Shadows I founded the band Ningizzia with my friend Stephane and we are still active even though there isn't much happening right now. Another band that I am involved in is Shubend and we are currently working on a new full-length. Forest of Shadows is definitely my main focus though.
Who are the musicians at the moment? I know that there were a lot of changes in the past. Are you still searching for other musicians?
I'm the only musician in the band right now and I'm not searching for another memeber at the moment.

How would you describe your music for the people who don't know your band? To me it seems that you have been influenced by bands like Opeth, Katatonia (On Brave Murder Day) and even My Dying Bride. Do you agree with that? Do you like this style of music?

You could probably find elements in the music of Forest of Shadows that hint at these three bands and they are all big influences. It would be a lie to say anything else. Still, there are so many other bands that inspire like for instance Shape of Despair, Cult of Luna, Anathema, Anekdoten and of course the late Katatonia. And yes, I do like the style, not that all of these bands are of similar style but well, they all do pretty dark and melancholic music.
Like bands in this genre, you're lyrics are full of emotion. Are they based on real life or just fiction? What can you tell me about it?

There may be some hints in the lyrics at stuff that has actually happened in real life but most of it is just fiction so don't take it to serious. I don't think you have to be sad or depressed in order to make dark music. You just have to like it. It's similar with lyrics. I would say that if you are really depressed then you don't have any energy to make any music or lyrics at all.

You made 4 promo's in the past if I'm correct. Only after the 2000 promo "Where Dreams Turned To Dust" you have been picked up by Rage Of Achilles. They also re-released the promo. Why did it took so long to find a label? Where they not interested?
I guess we weren't good enough both as a band and at sending promos to labels. The few labels that we actually did contact were obviously not interested.

I'm not familiar with the first 2 promo's. Do they sound like the 1999 en 2000 promo or something different? Could you tell me something more about them? Do you have the intention to re-release them in the future as a bonus? I think that there will be some interest in that material.

I know that alot of people want these early recordings and I do get alot of mail about this but I have no intention of re-releasing them at least not in their original shape. I mean they are just early versions of songs that deserve far better and they will be (and in some cases have been) released.
You have plans to release an album in 2003. What can you tell me about it? Is it a further development from the latest MCD? When will it be released?

Well, it will actually be released in 2004 since it has been pretty much delayed. The songs will be in the same vein as the mCD but with much more variation and diversity. It will have passages that are more aggressive than anything done by FoS before but it will also have some pretty soft passages. Overall, the atmosphere is more dark and emotional than before so hopefully the new elements will be appreciated. There is already samples and lyrics on the website for thoose who can't wait.
Do you have any plans for the future? Any touring plans or just see what happens?
As it is right now my only plan is to get this full-length finished 'cause it's killing me.
That's it, thanks for your time. Anything more to add to this interview?
Thanks for the interview and do check out the new album when it's out...

item.author: Jens
item.p: Wieso arbeitet ihr als Duo mit Session Musikern und habt keine festen Mitglieder fÃ¼r die Band rekrutiert?

DafÃ¼r gibt es mehrere GrÃ¼nde. Wir haben uns nach Musikern umgesehen, aber richtig geklappt hat es nie, entweder wegen mir oder wegen Micce. Mit mir und Micce kann man nur schwer arbeiten, und ich glaube, dass das der Grund ist, warum es zwischen uns nicht geklappt hat.
Was kannst du mir Ã¼ber die Vergangenheit der Band erzÃ¤hlen?

Das war schon eine sehr harte Zeit, wegen den vielen Wechseln im Line-Up und anderen Sachen wie Probleme mit Aufnahmen, Labels und Vertrieben. Es gab aber auch schÃ¶ne Zeiten, zum Beispiel als wir bei mp3.com an der Spitze waren und viel Geld verdienten (jaaa, ich bin ein gieriger Bastard). Der Tag, an dem unsere MCD erschien war auch ein groÃartiger Tag.
Wieso hat es so lange gedauert, bis ihr endlich einen Plattenvertrag unterschrieben hattet?

Wir haben nicht viele Demos an Zines und Labels geschickt und ich denke, wir mÃ¼ssen uns selbst dafÃ¼r die Schuld geben, dass wir nicht frÃ¼her gesignt wurden. Wir sind bei einem Label vor Rage Of Achilles untergekommen, dass aber wegen finanzieller Probleme dicht machte.
Wieso habt ihr euer Demo aus dem Jahr 2000 wiederverÃ¶ffentlicht? Hattet ihr nicht genÃ¼gend Material, das man stattdessen hÃ¤tte verÃ¶ffentlichen kÃ¶nnen?

Das Demo, dass 2000 aufgenommen wurde, war ursprÃ¼nglich als MCD gedacht. Wir hatten groÃe Hoffnungen, dass wir ein Label finden wÃ¼rden, dass es im Herbst 2000 noch verÃ¶ffentlichen wÃ¼rde. Aber leider hatten sich die Dinge nicht so entwickelt. Als wir bei Rage of Achilles unterschrieben, hatten wir genug Material, aber wir wollten einfach die MCD verÃ¶ffentlichen. Und Rage of Achilles hielten das ebenfalls fÃ¼r eine gute Idee.
Wo seht ihr musikalische EinflÃ¼sse auf euren Sound?

In anderen Bands, Metal- und Nicht-Metal-Bands, als auch in dÃ¼sterer Poesie und Filmen. Es gibt keine spezielle Band oder einen speziellen Poeten oder irgendetwas, das ich als Haupteinfluss bezeichnen wÃ¼rde.
Hat sich euer Sound seit damals, also 2000 in irgendeiner Art verÃ¤ndert?

Das hÃ¤ngt davon ab, was du mit "euer Sound" meinst. Die Musik ist immer noch genau so dÃ¼ster und depressiv wenn nicht sogar noch mehr. Die groÃen VerÃ¤nderungen betreffen die Verwendung der Instrumente und das Songwriting. Meiner Meinung nach ist das neue Zeug viel besser geschrieben und hoffentlich haben sich meine instrumentellen FÃ¤higkeiten auch verbessert.
Was kÃ¶nnen wir vom Full-Length-Album erwarten? Werden dort auch Ã¤ltere Songs drauf sein oder nur neues Material?

Es wird einen alten Song namens "November Dream" enthalten, der ursprÃ¼nglich auf unserem ersten Demo "The Silent Cry" erschien. Das restliche Material wurde nach der MCD geschrieben. Was ihr von dem neuen Album erwarten kÃ¶nnt, ist so ziemlich das gleiche wie auf der MCD nur extremer in die Metal- als auch in die Non-Metal-Richtung. Es wird noch dÃ¼sterer und epischer sein. Ich denke, ihr werdet es alle mÃ¶gen.
Wann kÃ¶nnen wir mit der VerÃ¶ffentlichung des Full Lengths rechnen?
Ich denke irgendwann im FrÃ¼hjahr/Sommer 2003.
Ich habe auf eurer Homepage gelesen, dass Micce die Band verlassen hat! Kannst du uns nÃ¤here Details dazu geben?

Also, wir beide hatten unsere Ideen und die finden sich nicht immer. Entweder hÃ¤tten wir auf einer Basis von Kompromissen, mit denen wir beide leben kÃ¶nnen, weiter gemacht oder wir hÃ¤tten uns getrennt. Ich dachte, Letztere wÃ¤re die bessere Option. Ich kann mich nicht selbst sehen, wie ich Zeit mit etwas verschwende, von dem ich weiÃ, dass ich viel besser machen kÃ¶nnte.
Ist die geplante Full-Length Platte jetzt in Gefahr?
Es wird wohl nur stark verspÃ¤tet erscheinen, als jetzt schon ist, da ich alles selbst machen muss, aber es ist definitiv nicht in Gefahr.
Siehst du Probleme darin, die Band alleine fortzufÃ¼hren?

Auf keinen Fall! Ich sah das kommen, da ich wusste, dass es zwischen mir und Micce nicht ewig klappen wÃ¼rde. Ich denke, ein Teil von mir war auf die Trennung und auf die Soloarbeit vorbereitet und deswegen war es fÃ¼r mich nichts neues.
Wird Micce jetzt seine eigene Band an den Start bringen?

DarÃ¼ber habe ich mit ihm noch nicht gesprochen, aber das wird er vielleicht machen. Er ist ein verdammt guter Musiker und hat viele interessante Ideen. Ich denke, er kann sehr interessantes Zeug vorlegen. Ich habe nie verstanden, warum er immer unbedingt mit mir arbeiten wollte. Es gibt soviel das er ohne mich besser machen kann.
Eure Texte sind sehr dÃ¼ster und depressiv. Wo bezieht ihr eure Inspirationen dafÃ¼r?
Einige Teile sind Reflektionen von sehr persÃ¶nlichen Erfahrungen meinerseits, wenn auch etwas dramatischer geschildert und andere Teile sind von dunkler Kunst generell beeinflusst.
MÃ¼ssen wir uns euch als depressive und melancholische Typen vorstellen - was man erwartet, nachdem man die Texte gelesen hat - oder ist das nur, weil diese Art besser zur Musik passt?

Ich war eine lange Zeit sehr down und depressiv, aber ich kann nicht sagen, dass ich noch so bin. Ich liebe immer noch dunkle und melancholische Kunst und ernsthaft kann ich mich auch nicht vorstellen, eines Tages frÃ¶hliche Musik zu machen.
Erachtest du den Erfolg bei mp3.com als eines der wichtigsten Teile im Puzzle eures Erfolges?
Es war sicherlich wegen mp3.com, dass wir unter Vertrag genommen wurden, also ist das in der Tat ein wichtiges Teil im Puzzle des Erfolges.

item.author: Bobo Nickolov
item.p: Hail Niclas.For the start ,can you present Forest of Shadows to the macedonian readers of IMMORTALITY?

Forest of Shadows was founded back in '97 as a contribution to the world of dark music. It was thus a solo-project at the beginning. After the release of the first demo "The Silent Cry", multi- instrumentalist Micce Andersson joined and gave the band a new fresh sound. This was first heard on the promo "Promo '99" which gained good critics in the underground. Due to increasing popularity the idea of playing live was born and it resulted in the recruitment of guitarist Martin Claesson. Another result of this new popularity was the recordings of a new promo "Promo 2000" which was meant to show even more signs of progression.

In the beginning of 2000 two gigs were held with the help of some session-musicans. These two gigs were far from satisfying and it was decided that focus should be on the recordings of a new demo which hopefully would lead to a record-deal and not on any more gigs. Before the recording-sessions took place Martin left the band due to several reasons. Even though he had been very much involved with the new material it was decided to record it without him. This new material resulted in the demo "where dreams turn to dust". The intention of this new demo was that it should be released as a mCD by some record company and so it was, though a little delayed due to various problems, by the english label Rage of Achilles in October 2001.
The deal with Rage of Achilles didn't just include the mCD but also a full-length and as we speak this full-length album is being recorded with a scheduled release in late 2002/early 2003.
At the begging you Forest of Shadows was a solo-project.What was the idea for making a solo-project like this one?
I just couldn't find any other musicians who shared my musical ambitions so I had to do it all by myself.
Can you tell me something more about your releases?
